messages.glossary.1933.context: Nazis (Nationalsozialisten) take power. End of the Weimar Republic.
messages.glossary.1945.context: End of WWII / Nazi rule. 8. Mai 1945 = end of war in Europe.
messages.glossary.1989.context: Fall of the Berliner Mauer (9. November). Beginning of Wiedervereinigung.
messages.glossary.1990.context: German reunification (3. Oktober) — now German Unity Day (Tag der Deutschen Einheit).
messages.glossary.Wahl.context: Election is the central instrument of democratic participation: through it, citizens determine who represents them in parliaments at local, state, and federal level. The Grundgesetz prescribes in Article 38 five principles for Bundestag elections — universal, direct, free, equal, and secret — that ensure each vote is equally weighted and no one can be pressured when casting their vote. Elections thus form the concrete expression of Volkssouveränität (popular sovereignty) in the democratic Rechtsstaat (constitutional state).
messages.glossary.Wahlen.context: Elections. Bundestag every 4 years; Landtag every 5; Kommunalwahlen at municipal level.
messages.glossary.nicht.context: Negation — flips the question. Always read negated questions twice before picking.
messages.glossary.Bundestag.context: The Bundestag (Federal Parliament) is the directly elected parliament of the Federal Republic of Germany. It passes laws, elects the Bundeskanzler, and controls the Bundesregierung. In its legislation, it is bound by the Grundgesetz—it may not infringe upon the essential content of fundamental rights.
messages.glossary.DDR.context: The German Democratic Republic (DDR, or East Germany) was the socialist German state that existed from 1949 to 1990 on the territory of the Soviet occupation zone. It was a one-party dictatorship under the leadership of the SED, in which fundamental rights such as freedom of opinion, press, and travel were severely restricted. With the peaceful revolution in 1989 and the reunification on October 3, 1990, the DDR joined the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.kann.context: 'Can/may'. Often signals a choice or possibility — read whether the question asks what is allowed.
messages.glossary.Berlin.context: After the Second World War, Berlin was divided into four occupation zones and became the central stage of the Cold War. While East Berlin served as the capital of the DDR (German Democratic Republic), West Berlin formed an enclave within the DDR and belonged to the Federal Republic of Germany. With the opening of the Wall in 1989 and reunification (Wiedervereinigung) in 1990, Berlin became the capital of a united Germany again.
messages.glossary.Gesetz.context: A law is a rule enacted by parliament that is generally binding and applies equally to all people. In a rule-of-law state, both citizens and state bodies are bound by the laws—no person and no authority stands above them. They thus form the framework that protects constitutional rights and governs how people live together in society.
messages.glossary.Partei.context: Political parties unite societal interests and participate in political decision-making, particularly in the Bundestag, where they introduce and enact laws. Their activity is limited by the Basic Law (Grundgesetz): initiatives that violate the constitution or fundamental rights are legally impermissible. As an expression of societal pluralism, parties compete politically and form a central element of parliamentary democracy.
messages.glossary.alle.context: 'All/every'. Watch for traps: 'all immigrants must…' usually wrong; 'all residents…' often right.
messages.glossary.Bundesland.context: A Bundesland (Federal State) is a constituent state of the Federal Republic of Germany with its own constitution, parliament, and government. Germany comprises 16 Bundesländer, which have independent responsibilities in areas such as education, police, and culture and participate in legislation at the federal level through the Bundesrat. After reunification in 1990, five new Bundesländer were established in the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic), which increased the total from ten to sixteen.
messages.glossary.darf.context: 'May / is allowed'. Marks a question about permission or right.
messages.glossary.Gericht.context: A court is a state institution that makes binding decisions on legal disputes and criminal cases according to applicable law. Courts form the judiciary—the third power in the state—and are intentionally separate from government and parliament. This judicial independence is a fundamental principle of the rule of law and is anchored in the Grundgesetz.
messages.glossary.Bundeskanzler.context: The Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor) is the head of government of the Federal Republic of Germany and leads the Bundesregierung (Federal Government). According to Article 63 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law), he is elected by the Bundestag (Federal Parliament) on the proposal of the Bundespräsident (Federal President). He holds policy direction authority under Article 65 of the Grundgesetz, meaning the right to set fundamental guidelines for government policy, and he bears responsibility for this to parliament.
messages.glossary.Grundgesetz.context: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) is the constitution of the Federal Republic of Germany and came into force on May 23, 1949. The name was deliberately chosen to emphasize the provisional character of the document during German division; after reunification in 1990, it was retained. It establishes fundamental rights, state principles, and the structure of constitutional organs, and serves as the legal standard by which all state actions are measured.
messages.glossary.muss.context: 'Must'. A strict requirement — careful: 'every citizen MUST vote' is wrong (it's a right).
messages.glossary.Gesetze.context: Laws (plural). Made by the Bundestag; the state and all residents must follow them.
messages.glossary.Verfassung.context: A constitution is the supreme legal document of a state: it establishes binding rules for the fundamental rights of people, the structure of the state, and the limits of state power. In Germany, the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) serves this function—it came into force in 1949 and binds all state powers to its requirements. Political parties that actively work against this constitutional order can be banned by the Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court).
messages.glossary.Richter.context: Judges are state-appointed officials of the judiciary who administer justice in the name of the state and are bound by law and legal principles. The Grundgesetz guarantees their personal and institutional independence, so they may not receive instructions from either the government or parliament. This judicial independence is a central feature of the rule of law and protects the judiciary from political influence.
messages.glossary.kein.context: 'No / not any'. A negation — flips what the question is asking for.
messages.glossary.nur.context: 'Only'. Almost always a trap word — 'only X' is rarely the correct answer.
messages.glossary.Bundespräsident.context: The Bundespräsident (Federal President) is the head of state of the Federal Republic of Germany and one of the five constitutional bodies. He represents the state internally and externally, acts as an integrating force in social and political conflicts, and provides impulses for public debate—without assuming government responsibilities. He is not elected directly by the people but by the Bundesversammlung, to deliberately keep the office separate from party-political disputes.
messages.glossary.Bundespräsidentin.context: The Federal President (female form). Same role as Bundespräsident.
messages.glossary.Sozialversicherung.context: Social insurance is a state-regulated system that protects people against basic life risks such as illness, old age, unemployment, need for care, and workplace accidents. It comprises five branches in Germany: health, pension, unemployment, long-term care, and accident insurance. The system is based on the solidarity principle, whereby contributions are normally shared by employers and employees, and all insured persons are entitled to benefits in case of need.
messages.glossary.Bundeskanzlerin.context: The Chancellor (female form). Head of the federal government.
messages.glossary.Mauer.context: Wall — specifically the Berliner Mauer (1961–1989) that split East and West Berlin.
messages.glossary.Nationalsozialisten.context: The Nazis — came to power 1933 under Hitler. Ended 1945.
messages.glossary.Meinungsfreiheit.context: Freedom of opinion is enshrined in Article 5 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects the right of every person to express and disseminate their views freely. It is a basic requirement for a functioning democracy because political competition and public oversight of state action are only possible if citizens can express their opinions without fear of state persecution. It finds its limits in general laws and in the protection of others' personal honor.
messages.glossary.keine.context: 'No / not any' (feminine form). A negation that flips the question.
messages.glossary.Wappen.context: A coat of arms is an official state emblem of a nation, federal state, or city and represents that state's identity and history to the outside world. The coat of arms of the Federal Republic of Germany features the Bundesadler (Federal Eagle) — a black eagle with red beak and red talons on a gold field — and thus connects to centuries-old tradition of governance. Federal states and cities have their own coats of arms, such as Berlin with its black bear on white field, which has served as the city symbol since the Middle Ages.
messages.glossary.Demokratie.context: In a democracy, all state power originates from the people: citizens exercise it through free, secret elections, through parliaments, and through independent courts. The Grundgesetz (German Constitution) anchors this principle in Article 20 and links it with separation of powers, protection of fundamental rights, and freedom of the press. Political parties bundle different societal interests and enable an orderly, peaceful change of political majorities.
messages.glossary.Fraktion.context: A Fraktion (parliamentary group) is a coalition of members of parliament from the same party or politically allied parties in the Bundestag; it organizes parliamentary work, coordinates shared positions, and introduces motions and bills. In the German Bundestag, a Fraktion must comprise at least five percent of members. The strength of a Fraktion determines both a party's influence in parliament and whether a government has a parliamentary majority.
messages.glossary.Bundesregierung.context: The Bundesregierung (Federal Government) consists of the Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor) and the Bundesminister (Federal Ministers) and is one of the five constitutional bodies of the Federal Republic of Germany. It directs the policy of the Bund, implements laws, and depends on a stable majority in the Bundestag for doing so. Its members are formally appointed and dismissed by the Bundespräsident.
messages.glossary.Bundesrat.context: The Bundesrat (Federal Council) is one of the five constitutional bodies of the Federal Republic of Germany and represents the interests of the 16 Bundesländer at the federal level. It consists of members of the Landesregierungen (State Governments), whose voting weight depends on the population of each state. For certain federal laws, the Bundesrat must approve or can lodge an objection—it is thus a central element of German federalism and secures the participation of the Länder in national legislation.
messages.glossary.Ehrenamt.context: Volunteer service refers to unpaid, voluntary work for the benefit of the community, such as in associations, social institutions, or the fire department. It forms an important foundation of social cohesion in Germany. A legally regulated exception exists for election assistance: German citizens can be required by authorities to participate in conducting elections.
messages.questions.21.imageCaption: Germany's Bundesflagge is three horizontal stripes: schwarz-rot-gold (black-red-gold), top to bottom.
messages.questions.55.imageCaption: The Bundesadler — a stylised black eagle facing right, on yellow shield. Germany's national emblem.
messages.questions.70.imageCaption: European Parliament chamber / EU symbol set — the official EU flag is 12 gold stars in a circle on blue.
messages.questions.130.imageCaption: EU flag: 12 gold stars in a circle on a blue field. The number 12 is fixed, not the number of member states.
messages.questions.176.imageCaption: Map of Germany after WWII showing the four Besatzungszonen (US, UK, France, Soviet).
messages.questions.181.imageCaption: DDR (East Germany) symbol: hammer-and-compass with a wreath of grain.
messages.questions.187.imageCaption: Map of the divided Germany — DDR (East) and BRD (West) with Berliner Mauer.
messages.questions.209.imageCaption: Bundesadler (federal eagle) — Germany's coat of arms.
messages.questions.216.imageCaption: EU flag: 12 gold stars in a circle on blue.
messages.questions.226.imageCaption: EU flag: 12 gold stars in a circle on blue. (Pick the one with stars, not stripes.)
messages.questions.235.imageCaption: Map of EU member states — the answer depends on which countries are highlighted.
messages.questions.301.imageCaption: Berlin's Wappen: black bear standing upright on a white shield, with a red mural crown above.
messages.questions.308.imageCaption: Map of Germany's 16 Bundesländer numbered on the map. Berlin is the small enclave inside Brandenburg.

messages.ui.theme.label: Theme
messages.questions.1.study.note: Public criticism of the government is a constitutionally guaranteed right in Germany, not a privilege. This right makes it possible to control government action socially and to discuss political alternatives openly.
messages.questions.2.study.note: The parental right to determine their children's religious education is anchored in the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) and applies until religious maturity at age 14. After that, young people can decide for themselves about their religious participation.
messages.questions.3.study.note: The principle of the rule of law explicitly includes the state itself: authorities and governments may only do what the law permits them to do—citizens are thus protected from arbitrary state action. In this way, the rule of law differs fundamentally from systems in which state power can be exercised without control.
messages.questions.4.study.note: The Basic Law guarantees a range of freedoms, including freedom of opinion as one of the central prerequisites for democratic life. Without the free expression of views, neither political participation nor public debate would be possible.
messages.questions.5.study.note: Free elections protect voters' decisions from any form of external influence: neither pressure nor coercion may direct voting, and no one may be disadvantaged because of their voting decision. This protection applies both before and after the election.
messages.questions.6.study.note: Germany is among the countries that have given their constitution a distinctive name: the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) carries this special designation because it was initially conceived in 1949 as a provisional document for the West German state.
messages.questions.7.study.note: Freedom of belief and conscience ranks among the oldest and most central fundamental rights of democratic states because it protects the innermost sphere of human conviction from state interference. The Basic Law explicitly anchors this right in Article 4 because Nazi rule had shown how dangerous it is when the state prescribes what people should think and believe.
messages.questions.8.study.note: The Basic Law guarantees fundamental rights such as dignity, freedom, and equality before the law, but does not prescribe an equal distribution of income or wealth. Economic equality of outcomes is not a constitutional mandate—the state protects people from existential hardship without interfering in free self-development through property ownership and freedom of occupation.
messages.questions.9.study.note: The right to asylum holds a special place among fundamental rights because by its nature it can only apply to foreigners—a German cannot seek asylum from the German state within Germany. All other fundamental rights apply either to all people or at least also to Germans.
messages.questions.10.study.note: The Basic Law protects human dignity and physical integrity as inalienable fundamental rights, which is why only sanctions are permissible that do not violate them. Financial penalties such as fines do not interfere with the person and are therefore compatible with the constitution.
messages.questions.11.study.note: Germany deliberately chose not to use the term 'constitution': the name 'Grundgesetz' (Basic Law) was intended to express the provisional nature of the document during the division of Germany. After reunification in 1990, the document retained this name, although it has long been regarded as a permanent constitution.
messages.questions.12.study.note: Even a parliamentary majority cannot abolish freedom of the press, because the Basic Law guarantees a core of fundamental rights that no state power may touch.
messages.questions.13.study.note: In the parliamentary system, opposition arises automatically from the election result: whoever does not belong to the governing majority sits on the opposition benches by definition. This principle ensures that every elected party in parliament either bears responsibility in government or controls it from outside.
messages.questions.14.study.note: Freedom of opinion applies regardless of the medium—whether spoken, written, or on the internet. Making a view public digitally is therefore just as constitutionally protected as expressing it in everyday conversation.
messages.questions.15.study.note: Article 12 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) protects freedom of occupation and explicitly includes a prohibition on forced labor. Only within the framework of a judicially imposed prison sentence may the state compel someone to work.
messages.questions.16.study.note: Freedom of opinion protects value judgments and opinions, but not false statements of fact about persons—because these damage the reputation of the person affected without a legitimate interest in their dissemination. Where fundamental rights collide, the Grundgesetz sets limits to individual freedom to protect the personality rights of others.
messages.questions.17.study.note: The equality principle in the Grundgesetz is explicitly directed at the state and its institutions—they may not arbitrarily treat citizens differently. This sets a constitutional limit on state arbitrariness that is enforceable in courts.
messages.questions.18.study.note: Article 1 holds a special position in the Grundgesetz because it not only proclaims a fundamental right but also establishes the value framework for all other articles. All state power is therefore primarily obligated to respect and protect human dignity.
messages.questions.19.study.note: Freedom of movement protects personal life planning by preventing the state from dictating where someone must live or establish residence. The free choice of place of residence thus expresses the state's non-interference in fundamental life decisions.
messages.questions.20.study.note: The Grundgesetz explicitly protects the free democratic basic order from efforts to abolish it—this includes any party that strives for dictatorship. The Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court) may ban such a party upon petition.
messages.questions.22.study.note: Since the founding of the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic) in 1949, Germany has had no monarchy; the Grundgesetz places a sovereign people in the place of a hereditary monarch, exercising power through elected institutions.
messages.questions.23.study.note: The German labor market is characterized by dependent employment: the large majority of working people are employed by a company or public institution as employees or civil servants, not as self-employed on their own account.
messages.questions.25.study.note: The 16 Bundesländer (federal states) of the Bundesrepublik lie exclusively within German state borders. Alsace-Lorraine lies in France and was never part of the Bundesrepublik.
messages.questions.26.study.note: The Grundgesetz establishes in Article 20 that the Bundesrepublik Deutschland is simultaneously constituted as democratic, social, and federal—these three principles together form the foundation of the state order and are inseparably connected.
messages.questions.27.study.note: The Grundgesetz establishes that Germany is organized as a federal state—in contrast to a unitary state, in which all power lies centrally with the federal government.
messages.questions.28.study.note: The Bundestagswahl (federal parliamentary election) is the most direct expression of popular sovereignty: only the enfranchised people—not party committees, parliaments, or other institutions—grant representatives their democratic mandate.
messages.questions.30.study.note: Press censorship is incompatible with the fundamental principles of democratic order because it prevents free opinion formation, which every democracy depends on. Free elections, separation of powers, and an independent press are essential characteristics of democracy — state media control, by contrast, is a feature of authoritarian systems.
messages.questions.31.study.note: When after an election no single party has a majority in parliament, several parties form a coalition in order to govern together.
messages.questions.32.study.note: The three state powers — legislature, executive, and judiciary — are anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and exercise sovereign authority. The press, by contrast, is a societal institution that stands outside the state and is therefore able to monitor its actions independently.
messages.questions.33.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) establishes the separation of state and church: the state may not establish a state church and may not favor one religion over others. Religious communities organize their internal affairs independently, without state interference.
messages.questions.34.study.note: With the end of the German Empire in 1918, Germany became a republic — a form of state that the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) enshrines to this day. The head of state, the Bundespräsident (Federal President), is elected, not determined by inheritance.
messages.questions.35.study.note: The social state does not finance its benefits from general taxes, but rather primarily through dedicated social contributions that flow directly to the respective insurance branch. This contribution principle ensures that revenue is used directly for the insured risks.
messages.questions.36.study.note: Health insurance protects people from the financial consequences of illness and is thus a concrete instrument of social security in everyday life. It ensures that medical care does not depend on personal wealth.
messages.questions.37.study.note: In 13 of the 16 Bundesländer (federal states), the highest office of the executive is called Ministerpräsidentin or Ministerpräsident — only the three city-states of Berlin, Hamburg, and Bremen use different titles.
messages.questions.38.study.note: Article 20 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) establishes that Germany is a democratic and social federal state — these three terms together describe the fundamental form of state. The combination of democracy, the social state principle, and federal order forms the constitutional foundation of the Federal Republic.
messages.questions.39.study.note: Federalism in Germany means that state power does not rest solely with the federal government, but is distributed between the federation and the Bundesländer (federal states) — each federal state therefore carries its own governmental responsibility. This autonomy allows the states to manage their assigned tasks such as education, policing, and culture themselves.
messages.questions.40.study.note: The three words Unity, Law, and Freedom form the core statement of the German national anthem and sum up the fundamental state values on which the Federal Republic is built.
messages.questions.41.study.note: The multi-party system ensures that no single political force determines all societal beliefs and interests alone. By having different parties compete for voters, political will formation remains open and pluralistic.
messages.questions.42.study.note: Legislation is a core task of parliament because only an elected people's representative body has the right to pass binding rules for everyone in the name of the population. This ensures that new laws are democratically legitimized and are not determined by individuals or the government alone.
messages.questions.43.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) does not permit banning a party based on mere political radicalism, but only when a party actively and deliberately acts against the constitutional order. The decisive standard is thus militant action against the constitution—not simply the advocacy of anti-constitutional opinions.
messages.questions.44.study.note: Germany distinguishes between directly elected people's representatives—such as members of the Bundestag (parliament)—and offices filled by special bodies. The Bundespräsident (Federal President) is deliberately elected by the Bundesversammlung (Federal Assembly), not by the people, so that the office remains above party politics and independent from election campaigns.
messages.questions.45.study.note: Long-term care insurance is a mandatory component of the German social insurance system and is therefore legally equivalent to health, pension, unemployment, and accident insurance. Anyone employed in Germany is automatically insured in all five branches of the social insurance system.
messages.questions.46.study.note: Roads and schools are typical examples of state-provided public services: the state provides infrastructure and educational facilities because these foundations of social life cannot be left to the free market.
messages.questions.47.study.note: The state finances tasks that serve the common good, such as security, infrastructure, and social protection. Leisure activities and vacation travel, on the other hand, are part of the private sphere and are not financed with public funds for individual citizens.
messages.questions.48.study.note: The five constitutional organs of Germany are established in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and exercise state power directly. A citizens' assembly, by contrast, is an instrument of municipal citizen participation without constitutionally anchored state power.
messages.questions.49.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) explicitly assigns cultural authority—and thus education policy—to the Länder (states), not to the federal government. Each Bundesland (state) therefore enacts its own education laws and curricula under its own responsibility.
messages.questions.50.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) commits Germany to the principle of the social state, which links economic freedom with social protection—this is precisely what characterizes the social market economy as the economic order of the Federal Republic.
messages.questions.51.study.note: The state's monopoly on force excludes private actors from unilaterally detaining persons—such interventions in personal freedom are reserved exclusively for state authorities and require a legal basis. Arbitrary arrests by non-state actors thus contradict the core of the democratic rule of law.
messages.questions.52.study.note: Article 20 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) establishes that all state power emanates from the people—the people is thus the sovereign from which all political legitimation in Germany is derived. This principle distinguishes the democratic order from systems in which power is claimed by a ruler, a party, or another institution.
messages.questions.53.study.note: The rule of law principle binds not only citizens but also—explicitly—the state itself to valid law. State bodies may act only on the basis of laws—unilateral action without a legal basis is impermissible.
messages.questions.54.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) recognizes only three branches of government: legislative, executive, and judicial. A 'directive' is none of these—it is not a constitutional term but a general word for an instruction or guideline.
messages.questions.55.study.note: The Reichstag building in Berlin is the most famous architectural symbol of German democracy and seat of the Bundestag (Federal Parliament). Its distinctive appearance—especially the glass dome—makes it immediately recognizable as the seat of the Bundestag in photographs.
messages.questions.56.study.note: In Germany, public order at the municipal level is the responsibility of the municipal administration, not the federal government or the Länder (states). The Ordnungsamt (municipal order office) is an executive authority that belongs directly to the municipal administration and enforces local rules in citizens' everyday lives.
messages.questions.57.study.note: This convention strengthens the connection between parliamentary majority and parliamentary leadership: whoever holds the most seats in the Bundestag typically also provides the person who leads and represents the parliament.
messages.questions.58.study.note: The appointment of federal ministers by the Bundespräsident (federal president) is a formal constitutional act that secures the government's state legitimacy—however, the substantive personnel decision lies with the Bundeskanzler (federal chancellor), who proposes the ministers.
messages.questions.59.study.note: Jewish life in present-day Germany dates back to Roman times: the first written evidence of Jewish settlements, such as in Cologne, comes from the 4th century. This roughly 1,700-year history makes the Jewish population one of the oldest continuous communities in Central Europe.
messages.questions.60.study.note: In the German constitutional system, state power is divided into three branches: legislature, executive, and judiciary. The Bundestag and Bundesrat (Federal Council) together belong to the legislative branch because both participate in the passage of federal laws—the Bundestag enacts them, the Bundesrat participates through approval or objection.
messages.questions.61.study.note: In a democracy, the highest political power does not lie with a monarch or a government, but with the people themselves. Article 20 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) therefore establishes that all state power emanates from the people.
messages.questions.62.study.note: Germany is a federal state in which the Länder (states) have their own parliaments and thus their own democratic legitimacy. The election of such a state parliament therefore has its own name, Landtagswahl (state parliament election)—as the counterpart to the Bundestagswahl (federal parliament election) at the national level.
messages.questions.63.study.note: Courts belong to the judiciary and administer justice independently—they do not execute laws but rather check whether law has been followed. Government, administration, and police, by contrast, form the executive branch because they carry out state decisions in everyday life.
messages.questions.64.study.note: The Grundgesetz establishes federalism as a foundational principle: state power is deliberately distributed across three levels—federal government, Länder, and municipalities—so that no single level dominates. Each of these levels carries independent responsibility for specific areas of citizens' lives.
messages.questions.65.study.note: The separation of powers divides the legislature and executive: the cabinet belongs to the executive and is formed by the Bundeskanzler(in) (federal chancellor) and the Bundespräsident (federal president)—not through a parliamentary vote.
messages.questions.66.study.note: Berlin and Munich are the two largest cities with the most significant Jewish communities in Germany. After reunification, Berlin once again developed into a central location for Jewish life, while Munich, as an economic center of Bavaria, also attracted a strong Jewish community.
messages.questions.67.study.note: Federalism in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) intentionally grants the Bundesländer (federal states) cultural authority, which includes school policy as a central area. Thus education remains a matter for the states, while the federal government is responsible for other policy areas such as foreign policy and defense.
messages.questions.68.study.note: The Grundgesetz explicitly establishes in Article 7 that the entire school system is subject to state supervision. This ensures that education is provided comprehensively and according to common principles.
messages.questions.69.study.note: In German federalism, municipalities are at the foundation of the state structure, directly below the state level, and thus closest to citizens in everyday life.
messages.questions.70.study.note: The right to propose a chancellor candidate is one of the few active political powers of the Bundespräsident (Federal President) — it connects his integrative function with the parliamentary procedure of government formation. The Bundestag (Federal Parliament) formally elects the chancellor, but the procedure always begins with the proposal from the President's office.
messages.questions.71.study.note: Berlin is the political working center of the Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor) because both the seat of government and parliament are located there. The close physical proximity of the Bundeskanzleramt (Federal Chancellor's Office) and Bundestag makes regular presence in Berlin politically necessary.
messages.questions.73.study.note: The distribution of seats in the Bundestag reflects the election result: the strongest factions hold the most representatives and thus shape parliamentary majority formation. CDU/CSU and AfD achieved the highest vote shares in the Bundestag election 2025 and have since formed the two largest factions.
messages.questions.77.study.note: The Bundeswehr (German Armed Forces) is the entirety of German military forces and is under the supervision of the Federal Minister of Defense and ultimately of the democratically elected parliament.
messages.questions.80.study.note: Interpretation of the Grundgesetz is reserved for the Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court) because no other state organ may authoritatively decide what the constitution means in a particular case. This secures the supreme status of the Grundgesetz over all other legal norms and state decisions.
messages.questions.81.study.note: The election of the Bundeskanzler by the Bundestag anchors the confidence of elected representatives as the foundation of every government. Government leadership is thereby directly bound to the democratically legitimized will of the population.
messages.questions.82.study.note: The Grundgesetz grants the Bundeskanzler a special position of power within the cabinet: as the only member directly elected by the Bundestag, he presides and determines the guidelines of joint policy through his directive authority.
messages.questions.83.study.note: The election of the Bundeskanzler by the Bundestag anchors parliamentary confidence as the foundation of every government — without a parliamentary majority the chancellor cannot take office.
messages.questions.84.study.note: The office of Bundespräsident is intentionally designed as a representational state office: he embodies the Bundesrepublik Deutschland (Federal Republic of Germany) internally and in relation to other states, without conducting government business. This separation between representation and political leadership distinguishes the Bundespräsidialamt (Federal President's Office) from an executive office such as that of the Bundeskanzler.
messages.questions.85.study.note: The Bundesrat (upper house) is not an elected parliament, but a body composed of members of the state governments—this way, the interests of the states flow directly into federal politics. This structure secures federalism by having the executives of the states, rather than their parliaments, represented at the federal level.
messages.questions.86.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) deliberately provides no direct election of the head of state by the people, so that the office of Bundespräsident (federal president) stands above party political disputes. The Bundesversammlung (federal assembly), convened specifically for this purpose, gives the election democratic legitimacy without turning it into a matter of public election campaigning.
messages.questions.87.study.note: In Germany, the head of state and head of government are deliberately separate offices: while the Bundeskanzler/Bundeskanzlerin (federal chancellor) directs the government, the Bundespräsident/Bundespräsidentin (federal president) as head of state primarily carries out representative and integrative duties.
messages.questions.88.study.note: The Grundgesetz enshrines the separation of powers among the legislature, executive, and judiciary so that no single body can exercise power without restraint. The opposition in the Bundestag (parliament) carries out a central control function in this: it questions government decisions, poses inquiries, and develops parliamentary counter-proposals.
messages.questions.89.study.note: In German parliamentarism, members of the same party join together to form a Fraktion (parliamentary group) to represent their political goals jointly and in a coordinated way. This organizational unit is the basis for the division-of-labor structure of the Bundestag, since committee seats and speaking time are allocated according to party group size.
messages.questions.90.study.note: The Grundgesetz anchors federalism as a state principle so that the states (Länder) can not only govern their own territory but also exert influence on national lawmaking. The Bundesrat is the constitutional body created for this purpose, in which the state governments directly participate in federal legislation, lodge objections, or give their consent.
messages.questions.91.study.note: The Bundesrat votes on many federal laws, and its majority ratios depend directly on which parties form the state governments. When a government changes in a state, the political balance in the Bundesrat can shift—which can ease or block the passage of federal legislation.
messages.questions.93.study.note: Proportional representation links a party's vote share directly to its representation in the Bundestag: whoever wins more second votes also sends more members to parliament.
messages.questions.95.study.note: Education is considered a mandatory state duty in Germany: the state not only provides schools but also requires parents to ensure their children attend classes regularly. This principle applies to nearly all children, regardless of origin or nationality.
messages.questions.96.study.note: Denying the Holocaust is punishable in Germany under Section 130 of the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code) because it violates the protection of human dignity and social peace. The legislature has set a penalty framework that allows for both imprisonment of up to five years and fines.
messages.questions.97.study.note: Anyone permanently employed in Germany automatically pays social insurance contributions—the employer deducts them directly from wages and forwards them along with their own share. This way, employees are protected against illness, old age, and unemployment without any action on their part.
messages.questions.98.study.note: Since members hold their mandate personally and are not bound to party group loyalty, they can change or leave their parliamentary group at any time—which can shift or eliminate the numerical majority on which a government depends.
messages.questions.99.study.note: Germany's social insurance system is financed on a parity basis: employers and employees share contributions so that the burden does not fall on one side alone. This principle of shared responsibility anchors social security as a joint task of business and workers.
messages.questions.100.study.note: Statutory social insurance protects mandatory members collectively against defined life risks and is regulated by law. Life insurance, by contrast, is a voluntary private contract based on individual provision that stands outside this state mandatory system.
messages.questions.101.study.note: Trade unions arose in response to the structural power imbalance between capital and labor: individual workers could scarcely negotiate working conditions effectively, while collective association provides them with stronger counterweight against employers. Through collective bargaining and the right to strike, they represent exclusively the interests of workers—not those of employers or the state.
messages.questions.102.study.note: Germany no longer has aristocratic titles or state honors — the Order of Merit with the Federal Cross of Merit (Bundesverdienstkreuz) as its most recognized rank is therefore the primary instrument through which the state publicly recognizes outstanding civil society engagement.
messages.questions.103.study.note: In German parliamentarism, a coalition is formed when multiple parties together form a governing majority — the Ampelkoalition (traffic light coalition) unites the SPD, FDP, and Bündnis 90/Die Grünen as three politically differently oriented partners in such an alliance.
messages.questions.104.study.note: Pregnancy must not be grounds for dismissal in Germany — the Maternity Protection Act (Mutterschutzgesetz) explicitly protects women from being dismissed because of known or suspected pregnancy. This protection applies from the beginning of pregnancy and requires the employer's knowledge.
messages.questions.105.study.note: Counting votes after the polling place closes is one of the core tasks of the polling board (Wahlvorstand) to which poll workers (Wahlhelferinnen und Wahlhelfer) belong. Only through this publicly conducted vote count is the election result determined transparently and verifiably.
messages.questions.106.study.note: Counting votes after the polling place closes is one of the core tasks of the polling board (Wahlvorstand): it ensures that the result is determined publicly, transparently, and verifiably. This step is legally regulated and is directly part of the democratic control principle.
messages.questions.107.study.note: The electoral term of the Bundestag (German federal parliament) is four years — after which citizens must decide anew on the parliament's composition. This regular renewal ensures democratic control by the people and prevents an elected majority from governing permanently without legitimate authorization.
messages.questions.108.study.note: Active voting rights in Bundestag (German federal parliament) elections require German citizenship and completion of the 18th year of life — both conditions must be met simultaneously. Anyone who lives permanently in Germany but lacks German citizenship is excluded from Bundestag elections.
messages.questions.109.study.note: The legislative term of the German Bundestag is four years; after that, voters must decide anew on the parliament's composition. This regular renewal of mandates ensures democratic control and prevents elected representatives from governing permanently without accountability.
messages.questions.110.study.note: The Bundestag (German federal parliament) is elected every four years — a term of office long enough to implement political initiatives but short enough to ensure regular democratic control by the population.
messages.questions.111.study.note: Germany derives from its historical responsibility for the Holocaust a special obligation to protect the State of Israel, which is expressed in the concept of Staatsräson (reason of state). Therefore, a public call for the destruction of Israel is not only morally incompatible with Germany's self-understanding but also criminally prohibited.
messages.questions.112.study.note: Ballot secrecy is one of the five constitutional principles of democratic elections in Germany and protects the political freedom of every individual. Only those who can vote without observation and without consequences are truly free in their decision.
messages.questions.113.study.note: In German electoral law, the majority principle applies: the party that receives the most votes wins the election. This principle ensures a clear democratic decision by the population.
messages.questions.114.study.note: Participation in democratic elections in Germany is a right, not an obligation—the state cannot force anyone to vote. This voluntariness is an expression of personal freedom guaranteed to everyone by the Grundgesetz (Basic Law).
messages.questions.115.study.note: The active right to vote secures for every eligible citizen participation in the democratic process of forming the collective will—specifically, the casting of a ballot. It is thus the most direct form of citizen participation in representative democracy.
messages.questions.116.study.note: The right to vote in Germany distinguishes two aspects of democratic participation: the ability to cast a ballot and the ability to run as a candidate. Those who are allowed to vote exercise the active right to vote.
messages.questions.117.study.note: The five-percent threshold prevents too many minor parties from entering Parliament and making government formation difficult. Only parties that receive at least 5 percent of the second votes are represented in the Bundestag (parliament).
messages.questions.118.study.note: Although Makkabi clubs cultivate Jewish cultural traditions, club membership is open to all people regardless of religious affiliation or origin. This reflects the principle that civil society organizations in Germany must not exclude anyone on the basis of their religion.
messages.questions.119.study.note: The principle of free choice protects political self-determination: no one may be pressured by the state or society to vote for a particular party or to participate in an election at all.
messages.questions.120.study.note: German electoral law combines two principles: the first vote determines, according to the majority voting system, who wins the constituency directly, while the second vote determines, according to proportional representation, the overall strength of the parties in the Bundestag (parliament). This combination is called personalized proportional representation.
messages.questions.121.study.note: German electoral law stipulates that only parties that receive at least five percent of the second votes nationwide enter Parliament—this regulation is known as the five-percent threshold. It prevents Parliament from fragmenting into too many small groups and thus ensures the state's capacity to govern.
messages.questions.122.study.note: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) stipulates in Article 38 that Bundestag (parliament) elections must be universal, direct, free, equal, and secret. The three principles of freedom, equality, and secrecy together protect the undistorted formation of political will by every individual.
messages.questions.123.study.note: The 5% threshold stipulates that a party must receive at least five percent of all votes cast to enter the Bundestag (German federal parliament). This protects the parliament from fragmentation into too many small factions, which would make stable majorities difficult.
messages.questions.124.study.note: The Bundestag is the only constitutional body directly elected by the people and thus forms the core of parliamentary democracy in Germany. As the elected parliament, it makes central political decisions at the federal level and provides governmental authority with its democratic legitimacy.
messages.questions.125.study.note: Regular elections prevent a government from remaining in power permanently without the support of the population. If it loses the support of the majority, the next election enables an orderly transfer of power without violence or arbitrary rule.
messages.questions.126.study.note: The mailing of voting notification is part of the state's duty to actively enable eligible voters to exercise their voting rights. This ensures that no one is excluded from democratic participation simply due to lack of information.
messages.questions.127.study.note: When many small parties are represented in parliament, several of them must form a coalition to achieve a governing majority—the more parties involved, the more difficult it becomes to agree on a common program. The 5% threshold prevents this fragmentation by denying entry into parliament to micro-parties.
messages.questions.128.study.note: In parliamentary democracy, the term 'representative' or 'member of parliament' refers to those elected by the people to serve in parliament and represent their interests. Election grants them democratic legitimacy, which is the decisive distinction from appointed or career state officials.
messages.questions.129.study.note: The Grundgesetz (German Basic Law) anchors popular sovereignty in Article 38: citizens elect the Bundestag directly—it is thus the only constitutional body that derives its democratic legitimacy directly from the people.
messages.questions.130.study.note: A ballot is valid if the voter's intent is clearly recognizable and no impermissible additions or reservations obscure the ballot. What matters is that the marking is done in such a way that there is no doubt about the intention of the person voting.
messages.questions.131.study.note: In Germany, the office of mayor is bound to the municipal level: the mayor serves as the administrative head of a municipality, not of a county, state, or the federal level.
messages.questions.132.study.note: Voluntary work is unpaid—those who do it act on their own initiative and without compensation. This free commitment in clubs and associations is a cornerstone of social life in Germany.
messages.questions.133.study.note: Eligible voters have mail-in voting available as a full alternative to in-person voting at the polling station for both federal and state elections. This right applies regardless of whether someone can appear in person on election day.
messages.questions.134.study.note: Municipal decisions such as eliminating a bus line can be influenced through organized citizen pressure, because municipal politicians depend on public opinion. Those who support an existing initiative or start one themselves pool voices and thereby increase pressure on the responsible agencies.
messages.questions.135.study.note: Trade unions arose as a counterforce to the economic dominance of employers in order to assert the interests of workers collectively. They negotiate on behalf of employees for wages and working conditions that then become binding for entire industries.
messages.questions.136.study.note: The Labor Court (Arbeitsgericht) is the appropriate institution when employees believe a dismissal is unlawful and wish to contest it. Anyone who believes they have been dismissed without legitimate grounds can file a lawsuit there within a statutory period.
messages.questions.137.study.note: Germany has a specialized court system for employment law conflicts—such as disputes over dismissals, wages, or working conditions—with the Labor Court (Arbeitsgericht) at its foundation. This specialization ensures that employment matters are handled quickly and with expert knowledge.
messages.questions.138.study.note: German labor law protects employees from unjustified dismissals—the Labor Court (Arbeitsgericht) is the competent authority to review the legality of a dismissal with binding effect.
messages.questions.139.study.note: A court trial requires that the public prosecutor formally charges a person—that is, formally raises the allegation that a specific crime was committed. Civil disputes, administrative decisions, or mere suspicions without formal charges do not lead to a criminal trial.
messages.questions.140.study.note: German criminal law combines professional judicial expertise with democratic citizen participation: lay judges (Schöffen) sit as equals alongside professional judges and participate in decisions on guilt and sentence. This principle of lay participation ensures that verdicts do not emerge from the professional apparatus alone, but incorporate societal values.
messages.questions.141.study.note: In the German legal system, the legal profession is an independent profession that combines both out-of-court consultation and courtroom representation. This combination of both tasks in one person ensures that legal assistance comes from a single source from the initial assessment through the verdict.
messages.questions.142.study.note: Judges are bound solely by law and deliver their verdicts independently of state or political directives. This separation of the judiciary from legislation and administration is a core element of the separation of powers anchored in the Grundgesetz (German Constitution).
messages.questions.143.study.note: Courts and judges belong to the judiciary because they neither pass laws nor assume administrative functions, but solely administer justice. This assignment to the third branch of government ensures their independence from politics and administration.
messages.questions.144.study.note: The Grundgesetz (German Constitution) divides state power into three independent branches: the legislative, the executive, and the judicial. Judges belong to the judicial branch because they neither pass laws nor execute them, but rather determine in specific cases whether applicable law has been observed.
messages.questions.145.study.note: Judges belong to the judicial branch because their task consists solely in administering justice—that is, applying laws to specific cases. The other two branches, the legislative and executive, are strictly separated from this task by the principle of separation of powers.
messages.questions.146.study.note: In the German legal system, a formal proceeding before a court is called a Prozess (trial)—a term that encompasses both civil and criminal proceedings. The term is firmly established in legal usage and refers to the orderly, rule-of-law resolution of conflicts by independent judges.
messages.questions.147.study.note: In the German state system, courts have the exclusive task of administering justice—that is, deciding with binding effect in specific cases who is in the right. This task is strictly separate from lawmaking by parliament and the work of the executive branch.
messages.questions.148.study.note: The binding force of law on all state agencies is a core feature of the Rechtsstaat (constitutional state) — the police implement this principle in daily life by ensuring that applicable rules are followed. In doing so, they fulfill a fundamental protective function for public order and the rights of citizens.
messages.questions.149.study.note: Denying the Holocaust is a direct downplaying of a historically documented crime and thus attacks the dignity of the victims and the Jewish community. Such behavior is therefore a concrete expression of antisemitism.
messages.questions.150.study.note: The Schöffenamt (lay judge office) is a form of volunteer judicial activity: lay judges (Schöffen) perform their judicial duties without payment and without professional legal training.
messages.questions.151.study.note: The Wall was an instrument used by the DDR (East German state) leadership to forcibly prevent the ongoing exodus of population to the Bundesrepublik (West Germany). Its construction proceeded without public announcement or consultation, reflecting the authoritarian character of the DDR state.
messages.questions.152.study.note: Nazi rule began in January 1933 with Hitler's appointment as Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) and ended in May 1945 with German defeat in the Second World War. These twelve years left destruction and millions of victims in Germany and Europe and formed the foundation for the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) of 1949 as a deliberate rejection of dictatorship.
messages.questions.153.study.note: May 8, 1945 marks the unconditional surrender of the German Wehrmacht (armed forces) and thus the end of Nazi rule in Europe. In Germany, this date is now commemorated as the Day of Liberation from Nazism.
messages.questions.155.study.note: Nazi rule began in 1933 with Adolf Hitler's appointment as Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) and ended in 1945 with Germany's military defeat in the Second World War. These twelve years shaped German history so profoundly that the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) of 1949 was deliberately framed to address the failures of this era.
messages.questions.156.study.note: On January 30, 1933, Reichspräsident (Reich President) Paul von Hindenburg appointed Adolf Hitler as Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) — a date that marks the turning point from the Weimarer Republik (Weimar Republic) to Nazi dictatorship. Within a few months of this power transfer, democratic institutions, basic rights, and political parties were systematically dismantled.
messages.questions.157.study.note: With Hitler's appointment as Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) on January 30, 1933 and the subsequent Ermächtigungsgesetz (Enabling Act), the Nazis dismantled all democratic institutions within a few months and established a totalitarian one-party regime. The Weimarer Republik (Weimar Republic), Germany's first parliamentary democracy, was thereby completely eliminated.
messages.questions.158.study.note: With Hitler's appointment as Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) in 1933, parliament, political pluralism, and basic rights were systematically abolished and all state power concentrated in a single hand. This elimination of all democratic and constitutional controls made what was called the Drittes Reich (Third Reich) a dictatorship.
messages.questions.159.study.note: The Nazi dictatorship abolished democratic principles such as political competition, opposition, and popular sovereignty — free elections were thus structurally impossible. Votes during the Nazi period served to legitimize the regime, not to express the free will of the people.
messages.questions.160.study.note: The Second World War began with Germany's attack on Poland on September 1, 1939 and ended in Europe on May 8, 1945 with the surrender of the German Wehrmacht (armed forces) — six years in total that fundamentally changed Europe and the world.
messages.questions.161.study.note: In the NS state, racism was not a marginal phenomenon but the foundation of state action: laws, authorities, and institutions were oriented toward persecuting and destroying people according to racist criteria. This state racism distinguishes the NS dictatorship from other authoritarian systems and explains why the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) anchors human dignity and the prohibition of discrimination so centrally.
messages.questions.162.study.note: Claus Schenk Graf von Stauffenberg was a Wehrmacht officer who became the central organizer of military resistance against the NS regime. He personally placed the bomb at Hitler's headquarters on July 20, 1944, and stands today as a symbol of active resistance within the German armed forces.
messages.questions.163.study.note: The Night of Broken Glass of November 9–10, 1938, marks a turning point in Nazi persecution policy: for the first time, state-organized violence openly and nationwide targeted Jewish institutions and businesses.
messages.questions.164.study.note: The events of November 9, 1938 – the so-called Reichspogromnacht (Night of Broken Glass) – were not a spontaneous uprising but a state-directed act of violence against the Jewish population throughout the German Reich. Beyond synagogues, Jewish businesses were destroyed and looted, while authorities watched passively or actively participated.
messages.questions.165.study.note: Konrad Adenauer was the first Bundeskanzler (Chancellor) of the Bundesrepublik Deutschland (Federal Republic of Germany) from 1949 to 1963 and significantly shaped the country's early democracy. His term coincided with the reconstruction after World War II and the Western integration of the young republic.
messages.questions.166.study.note: The chant 'We are the people' was the defining slogan of the Monday demonstrations in the DDR in 1989 and expressed citizens' claim to popular sovereignty against the SED (Socialist Unity Party) dictatorship. With these words, the demonstrators demanded that political power should emanate from the people—a fundamental principle of democracy that the DDR leadership had suppressed until then.
messages.questions.167.study.note: The four Allied powers of World War II – the USA, Soviet Union, Great Britain, and France – jointly formed the occupying powers because only they possessed the military and political authority to administer defeated Germany. No other country assumed this formal responsibility for German territory after 1945.
messages.questions.168.study.note: Although Japan was also a wartime enemy of the Allies and was occupied by the USA after its capitulation in August 1945, it played no role in the occupation of Germany. The four Allied occupying powers in Germany were exclusively the USA, Soviet Union, Great Britain, and France.
messages.questions.170.study.note: The Nazi dictatorship eliminated political competition from 1933 onward by banning all parties except the NSDAP and replacing the principle of popular sovereignty with a one-party system.
messages.questions.171.study.note: The social market economy lets prices and quantities be determined by market mechanisms while simultaneously obligating the state to cushion social hardships – a balance anchored in the Sozialstaatsprinzip (social state principle) of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). In this way, it distinguishes itself from both an unregulated market economy and a state-directed planned economy.
messages.questions.172.study.note: The ideological and power-political orientation of the Soviet occupation zone laid the groundwork from the outset for a socialist state modeled on Moscow. Thus, the founding of the DDR (German Democratic Republic) in 1949 in this zone was not a random decision but the direct result of Soviet administrative policy.
messages.questions.173.study.note: Germany signed the Treaty of Rome in 1957 together with France, Italy, Belgium, the Netherlands, and Luxembourg – six states whose union developed over decades into today's European Union. Membership from the beginning shaped the political and institutional design of the EU substantially.
messages.questions.175.study.note: The four Allied powers of World War II – the USA, Great Britain, France, and the Soviet Union – decided at the Potsdam Conference in 1945 to jointly administer Germany, which is why the number of occupation zones corresponds exactly to the number of Allied powers.
messages.questions.176.study.note: The four victorious powers divided Germany geographically so that Britain took the northwest, the Soviet Union took the east, the United States took the south, and France took the southwest. Berlin, as the former capital, was likewise divided among all four powers and lay in the middle of the Soviet zone.
messages.questions.177.study.note: Berlin was of special symbolic and strategic importance as the capital of the defeated German Reich, which is why all four victorious powers insisted on joint administration of the city. This created the unique situation in which Berlin—although it lay in the middle of the Soviet occupation zone—was itself divided into four sectors.
messages.questions.178.study.note: The Soviet blockade of all land and water routes to West Berlin forced the western Allies to supply the city exclusively by air. Since no other connection to the besieged city remained open, establishing the airlift was the only way to ensure the supply of the population.
messages.questions.179.study.note: Unconditional surrender meant that Germany ended the war not through negotiations but through complete military defeat. This transferred unrestricted decision-making power over Germany's future to the Allies.
messages.questions.180.study.note: Konrad Adenauer was elected the first Bundeskanzler (federal chancellor) of the Bundesrepublik Deutschland (Federal Republic of Germany) in 1949 and shaped the young democracy through his long tenure lasting until 1963, his Western integration policy, and Germany's reintegration into the international community.
messages.questions.181.study.note: Brandt's kneeling was not a protocol act but a personal plea for forgiveness to the victims of Nazi terror—particularly the Polish Jews murdered in the Warsaw Ghetto. This gesture was thus directed at Poland as a nation and at the Jewish population that had suffered under German occupation.
messages.questions.182.study.note: Jewish communities gather for prayer and religious life in the synagogue, which thus forms the central house of worship of Judaism.
messages.questions.183.study.note: The economic upswing of the Bundesrepublik took place in the 1950s and fundamentally changed everyday life for the population—from housing shortages and rationing to increasing consumption and full employment. This decade is therefore considered the core phase of the Wirtschaftswunder (economic miracle).
messages.questions.184.study.note: The founding of the State of Israel in 1948 was based on Resolution 181 of the UN General Assembly from 1947, which established the partition plan for the British Mandate territory of Palestine. Thus, the international legal legitimation was not the result of a war or a unilateral declaration, but of a multilateral decision of the international community.
messages.questions.185.study.note: The term described the strict isolation of the Soviet-dominated Eastern Bloc from Western Europe—a border that was maintained not only militarily but also through travel and information restrictions. The Warschauer Pakt (Warsaw Pact) thereby formed the institutional backbone of this isolation policy.
messages.questions.186.study.note: The popular uprising of 17 June 1953 was a national holiday in West Germany for decades, commemorating the resistance of the DDR (East Germany) population against the SED (Socialist Unity Party) regime. With reunification in 1990, 3 October became the new national holiday as the Day of German Unity, and 17 June lost its holiday status.
messages.questions.187.study.note: The DDR adopted the black-red-gold colors of the German democratic movement, but added its state coat of arms to the flag in 1959—as a deliberate sign of distinction from the Bundesrepublik, which carried a plain black-red-gold flag without insignia.
messages.questions.188.study.note: The construction of the Berlin Wall in August 1961 marked the peak of German division: the DDR (German Democratic Republic) sealed in its citizens because hundreds of thousands had fled to the West in the years before.
messages.questions.189.study.note: The construction of the Wall in August 1961 was the response of the DDR (German Democratic Republic) leadership to continuing mass flight: by then, over three million people had left the DDR, which threatened to destabilize the country economically and politically.
messages.questions.191.study.note: The opening of the Berlin Wall on 9 November 1989 was the immediate result of growing pressure from the peaceful revolution movement in the DDR (German Democratic Republic). This ended the physical separation of the German population after 28 years.
messages.questions.192.study.note: As part of the Soviet occupation zone after 1945, Brandenburg belonged to the territory that would become the DDR (German Democratic Republic) and was admitted to the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic) as one of the five new Bundesländer after reunification in 1990.
messages.questions.193.study.note: The division of Berlin by a wall was an expression of the political and ideological split in Europe during the Cold War between the Western and Eastern power blocs.
messages.questions.194.study.note: October 3 stands as the national holiday for the conclusion of German state unity after decades of division.
messages.questions.195.study.note: After reunification in 1990, five new Bundesländer were created on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic) — including Sachsen-Anhalt, which comprises the historical territories of Sachsen and Anhalt. The other four new Länder are Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Sachsen, and Thüringen.
messages.questions.196.study.note: The term 'Wende' (turning point) describes the political upheaval in the DDR in the autumn of 1989, when mass demonstrations and the collapse of SED rule paved the way from a one-party dictatorship to a democratic system. This transformation led to free elections and finally to reunification with the Bundesrepublik Deutschland (Federal Republic of Germany).
messages.questions.197.study.note: After reunification in 1990, five Bundesländer were newly founded on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic) — including Thüringen, which had existed as a Land before German division and was dissolved by the DDR in 1952.
messages.questions.198.study.note: Of today's 16 Bundesländer, five are located on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic) — including Sachsen, which was newly founded as a Freistaat in 1990. The other four former DDR Länder are Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Sachsen-Anhalt, and Thüringen.
messages.questions.199.study.note: 'Stasi' was the colloquial short form for the Ministry for State Security, formed from the first syllables of the official name and was commonly used in everyday speech in the DDR.
messages.questions.200.study.note: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern lies in the northeast of Germany and is one of the five Bundesländer that were newly founded on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic) after reunification in 1990. The other four are Brandenburg, Sachsen, Sachsen-Anhalt, and Thüringen.
messages.questions.201.study.note: During reunification in 1990, exactly five new Bundesländer were created on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic): Mecklenburg-Vorpommern, Brandenburg, Sachsen, Sachsen-Anhalt, and Thüringen. East Berlin did not become a separate Bundesland but merged with West Berlin to form the present-day Bundesland Berlin.
messages.questions.202.study.note: The DDR (German Democratic Republic), as a socialist state, was closely bound to the Soviet Union and thus automatically part of the Eastern military alliance. The Warsaw Pact represented the institutional incorporation of the DDR into the Eastern Bloc and obligated it to military cooperation under Soviet leadership.
messages.questions.203.study.note: The DDR followed the Soviet economic model, in which the state centrally directed all economic decisions and largely prohibited private ownership of the means of production.
messages.questions.204.study.note: The reunification in 1990 did not take place through a merger of two equal states, but rather through the DDR's accession to the existing Federal Republic under Article 23 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). The five newly established eastern states formed the legal framework for this accession.
messages.questions.205.study.note: The DDR's accession to the Federal Republic on 3 October 1990 was not merely a domestic German process: since the Federal Republic was already a member of the European Community, the new federal states automatically became part of this European legal and economic area.
messages.questions.206.study.note: Stolpersteine (stumbling stones) make the abstract scale of Nazi terror visible through individual fates and permanently anchor the memory of the persecuted and murdered in public space. This form of commemoration aligns with Germany's constitutional mandate to protect the dignity of every individual and never forget the crimes of the dictatorship.
messages.questions.207.study.note: As a socialist state, the DDR was militarily integrated into the Eastern Bloc and belonged to the Warsaw Pact—the counterpart to the Western NATO, which the Federal Republic of Germany joined.
messages.questions.208.study.note: The Staatssicherheitsdienst (State Security Service) was a central instrument of power in the DDR dictatorship: it secured the rule of the SED (Socialist Unity Party) through comprehensive surveillance and systematic suppression of all political opposition.
messages.questions.209.study.note: The DDR's coat of arms featured a hammer and compass—symbols for workers and intellectuals—surrounded by a wreath of grain, which visually expressed the socialist state ideology of a workers' and peasants' state. It deliberately differed from the West German Bundesadler (federal eagle) and was meant to embody a distinct national identity for the DDR.
messages.questions.210.study.note: On 17 June 1953, strikes began among construction workers in East Berlin, which within hours spread to hundreds of thousands of people throughout the DDR and became an open uprising against the SED regime. Soviet tanks and troops violently suppressed the uprising—in the Federal Republic, 17 June was subsequently declared a national holiday as a sign of solidarity with the DDR population.
messages.questions.211.study.note: Willy Brandt, Federal Chancellor from 1969 to 1974, was the driving political force behind the Eastern Treaties and made rapprochement with the East the centerpiece of his chancellorship. For this détente policy, he received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1971.
messages.questions.215.study.note: As Federal Chancellor, Helmut Kohl was largely responsible for the reunification process and decisively advanced the diplomatic and domestic political steps toward uniting both German states.
messages.questions.216.study.note: The Bundesadler hangs as a gilded relief behind the presidium in the plenary chamber, making visible that parliament acts in the name of the German state. Its presence at this location directly connects the federal state's symbolism with the site of democratic lawmaking.
messages.questions.218.study.note: From the former DDR, exactly five new federal states emerged at reunification in 1990: Brandenburg, Mecklenburg-Western Pomerania, Saxony, Saxony-Anhalt, and Thuringia. These five states joined the Federal Republic and increased the total number of federal states from eleven to sixteen.
messages.questions.219.study.note: With the Two Plus Four Treaty of 1990, Germany recognized its present-day external borders as binding under international law and at the same time regained its full state sovereignty. Since then, these borders have been immutably fixed.
messages.questions.220.study.note: On 27 January 1945, Soviet troops liberated the extermination camp Auschwitz-Birkenau and thereby revealed the extent of Nazi mass murders. Germany has observed this date as a national memorial day since 1996 to remember the millions of victims of Nazi rule and maintain historical responsibility.
messages.questions.221.study.note: The abolition of border controls at internal frontiers is the concrete everyday benefit of the Schengen Agreement: travelers cross borders between member states without being stopped or asked for documents. Germany has belonged to the Schengen Area since 1995, so that its citizens enjoy this travel freedom in much of Europe.
messages.questions.228.study.note: The term 'German reunification' stands for the state and societal unity that was restored in 1990 after four decades of division. It emphasizes that this was not an incorporation or annexation, but rather the growing together of two previously separated parts of one people.
messages.questions.231.study.note: The term 'European integration' describes the path toward merger, whose institutional result is the EU. Both—process and result—belong together: integration is the path, the EU is the goal.
messages.questions.232.study.note: The European Parliament election is the only election in which EU citizens directly decide on the composition of a European institution — in contrast to other EU institutions whose members are determined by member states or other organs.
messages.questions.234.study.note: Strasbourg was chosen as the official seat because the city lies on the German-French border and thus symbolizes reconciliation between the formerly hostile neighboring countries. Besides Strasbourg, the Parliament also works in Brussels and Luxembourg.
messages.questions.235.study.note: Shared commemoration at a place representing the most devastating battles of the First World War symbolically expressed what drove European integration from the beginning: overcoming the centuries-old contrast between France and Germany in order to permanently anchor peace and security.
messages.questions.237.study.note: The jubilee year 2007 marked fifty years since the signing of the Treaties of Rome, with which the EEC (European Economic Community) was created as the first major step toward Europe's economic integration. The founding of the EEC formed the core of these treaties — not a political union or military alliance.
messages.questions.238.study.note: The European Parliament is distributed across three cities: the official seat is in Strasbourg, most committees and additional plenary sessions take place in Brussels, and the General Secretariat is located in Luxembourg.
messages.questions.239.study.note: The Federal Republic of Germany acceded to the Treaties of Rome in 1957 together with five other Western European states and thereby committed itself to economic and legal integration within the newly founded European Economic Community.
messages.questions.241.study.note: Social benefits such as parental allowance are not automatically granted in Germany but require a formal application from those entitled to them. Only through the application does the responsible authority examine the eligibility conditions and approve the benefit.
messages.questions.242.study.note: Attendance at kindergarten in Germany — unlike school — is voluntary, so only the parents or legal guardians decide whether and where their child goes. Parental educational rights protect this decision as a private family matter against state requirements.
messages.questions.243.study.note: Anyone wishing to organize a demonstration on public streets or squares in Germany is legally required to notify the responsible authority beforehand. The notification requirement applies to assemblies in the open air and serves to coordinate between organizers, authorities, and the public.
messages.questions.245.study.note: In Germany, the law protects minors from relationships with significant age gaps: a partnership between a child under 14 and an adult is legally not permitted. The relationship between Anne (13) and Tim (25) as a couple therefore violates the legal protection of children from sexual exploitation.
messages.questions.247.study.note: Maternity protection combines constitutional protection of marriage and family with the employment law principle of equal rights by ensuring that women do not suffer professional or economic disadvantages due to pregnancy.
