messages.questions.248.study.note: In Germany, the family bears primary responsibility for raising children — the state supports and supplements this role but does not take the lead. Schools, daycare centers, and Jugendämter (youth welfare offices) provide accompanying support without replacing parental responsibility.
messages.questions.249.study.note: In Germany, responsibility for raising children rests primarily with parents — not with the state, school, or other institutions. This task begins in the everyday life of the family and forms the foundation for a child's development.
messages.questions.250.study.note: In the German job market, educational qualifications decisively influence income and employment opportunities because employers expect demonstrable professional skills for qualified positions.
messages.questions.251.study.note: In Germany, children have a legally protected right to non-violent upbringing — physical punishment is forbidden, regardless of whether it is inflicted by parents or other caregivers. Anyone who hits a child violates this right and can be held criminally and civilly liable.
messages.questions.252.study.note: Monogamy is a principle of German family law: marriage is legally only possible if no existing marriage is in place. This principle applies regardless of cultural or religious traditions that recognize other forms of cohabitation.
messages.questions.254.study.note: The separation year serves as proof that the marriage has truly failed: only when both partners have lived apart for at least twelve months can a court pronounce the divorce. This period protects against hasty decisions and gives spouses time to consider reconciliation.
messages.questions.255.study.note: In Germany, parenting issues are regarded as the responsibility of public child and youth services, not the general welfare office or schools. The Jugendamt (youth welfare office) is the designated state agency for this purpose.
messages.questions.256.study.note: The restaurant license is an instrument of state trade law: the state makes the opening of a catering business conditional on official approval to ensure protection of guests and the public. Only after this license is granted can a business legally commence operations — regardless of whether it is run by individuals or married couples.
messages.questions.257.study.note: In Germany, adults have access to the second educational pathway, which allows educational qualifications to be obtained outside regular school hours. Evening grammar school is specifically designed to prepare adults for the Abitur (German university entrance qualification) in a work-compatible framework.
messages.questions.258.study.note: Physical violence against children and neglect resulting in hunger are considered child endangerment — a legal threshold at which the state places its duty to protect the child above parental rights. In such cases, the Jugendamt (youth welfare office) is authorized to remove the child from the family environment.
messages.questions.259.study.note: The BIZ (career information center) actively supports the search for an apprenticeship by providing information about open apprenticeships and accompanying the application process. The search for apprenticeships is thus one of the concrete placement tasks that the BIZ takes on as part of state-provided vocational guidance.
messages.questions.260.study.note: The requirement to attend school complements compulsory education: whoever must attend school must also appear there regularly. Unexcused absence from classes is not permitted in Germany and can have legal consequences.
messages.questions.261.study.note: In Germany, the Second Education Pathway is open to people who wish to complete a school diploma in adulthood. The evening secondary school (Abendgymnasium) is the institution that specifically admits working adults and enables them to obtain the Abitur.
messages.questions.262.study.note: The principle of equal treatment protects concretely against discrimination based on personal characteristics — including explicitly disability. In this way, the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) ensures that state authorities and the law treat no one worse solely because of physical or mental limitations.
messages.questions.263.study.note: With criminal responsibility starting at age 14, German law takes into account the judgment capacity of young people: whoever breaks laws at this age can be held legally accountable and sanctioned under juvenile law.
messages.questions.264.study.note: Wearing colorful costumes and masks is a central feature of Carnival, which reaches its festive peak on Rosenmontag (Rose Monday). The custom traces back to ancient traditions in which people could playfully reverse the social order through disguise.
messages.questions.265.study.note: In Germany, every marriage must necessarily begin with a visit to the registry office (Standesamt), because only the ceremony performed there has legal validity. Church or other ceremonies may take place afterward, but have no independent legal value.
messages.questions.266.study.note: In Germany, quiet hours begin at 10 p.m. and end at 6 a.m. This period is a nationwide guideline during which noise sources in private and public areas must be reduced so that the population's right to rest is preserved.
messages.questions.267.study.note: The right to choose one's partner freely is part of personal self-determination, which the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) protects. After their child reaches adulthood, parents have no legal means to influence their child's life choices.
messages.questions.268.study.note: The driving license theory test in Germany can be taken in more than ten languages to remove language barriers. This ensures that driving fitness and traffic knowledge — not German language skills — determine the test result.
messages.questions.269.study.note: The right to a kindergarten place is anchored in the Eighth Social Code (SGB VIII) and obligates youth welfare providers to provide sufficient places. It applies from the completion of the third year of life and ends with school enrollment.
messages.questions.270.study.note: The Volkshochschule (adult education center) is geared toward adults who wish to continue learning after their initial education — whether for professional development or personal education. Its municipal governance ensures low-threshold access for all social groups.
messages.questions.272.study.note: German family law permits marriage only between two people — simultaneous polygamy contradicts this principle and is prohibited. Other forms of living arrangements such as unmarried partnerships or single parents are, however, legally recognized and protected.
messages.questions.273.study.note: In Germany, the state shares responsibility for children's development, which is why a municipal agency called the Jugendamt (youth welfare office) exists specifically for this purpose. Families do not have to solve parenting problems alone but can access state-funded support.
messages.questions.274.study.note: Intentionally opening a letter addressed to another person violates that person's protected privacy. This right applies not only against the state but also protects against interference by other private individuals.
messages.questions.275.study.note: In a divorce in Germany, the so-called Anwaltszwang (mandatory legal representation requirement) applies: at least one of the two parties must be represented by a lawyer before the family court, and without this support the proceedings cannot take place.
messages.questions.276.study.note: The right to lodge a complaint with agency management expresses the rule of law principle that state action must be subject to control. Anyone who feels unjustly treated by an employee of a government agency can use this internal procedure without immediately taking legal steps.
messages.questions.277.study.note: During hiring, a candidate's marital status or parenthood must not be considered because the AGG (General Equal Treatment Act) prohibits discrimination unrelated to job qualifications. Rejection based solely on motherhood violates the equal treatment principle and constitutes direct discrimination based on sex.
messages.questions.278.study.note: A disability such as needing a wheelchair is not a valid reason to reject someone for office work — professional qualifications as an accountant are not affected by it. Anyone who is nonetheless rejected solely because of the disability experiences prohibited unequal treatment under the General Equal Treatment Act.
messages.questions.280.study.note: In German administrative law, objection (Einspruch) is the prescribed legal remedy against decisions from tax authorities — it must be filed before any court proceedings. Anyone who skips this step loses the right to challenge the decision later.
messages.questions.281.study.note: A swimming pool that rejects people based solely on skin color violates the prohibition on discrimination — both under Article 3 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) and under the General Equal Treatment Act, which protects access to public facilities and services.
messages.questions.282.study.note: Although volunteering in Germany is generally voluntary, election poll worker service is a legally established exception in which citizens can be required to participate. Democratic elections require a reliable workforce, which is why this service cannot depend solely on volunteers.
messages.questions.283.study.note: In German administrative law, citizens have the objection procedure (Widerspruchsverfahren) available against erroneous decisions before a court must be involved. An incorrect bill from a government agency is such a challengeable decision, against which objection is the legally prescribed first remedy.
messages.questions.284.study.note: Because workplace requirements constantly change through technological development, a single occupational qualification is no longer sufficient. Lifelong learning is therefore a recognized societal necessity — even for adults already in the workforce.
messages.questions.285.study.note: Employees with permanent contracts pay income tax and social insurance contributions directly from their salary—that is, direct deductions that the employer forwards to the state and insurance carriers. Sales tax, by contrast, arises when goods are purchased and is not deducted from wages.
messages.questions.286.study.note: Under German labor law, employees have the right to elect their own representative within their workplace to advocate for their interests against the employer. The Betriebsrat (works council) is precisely this internal point of contact—it operates directly within the company, whereas unions act across sectors at the industry level.
messages.questions.287.study.note: Anyone wishing to end an employment relationship in Germany cannot simply stop from one day to the next—termination must be communicated to the employer in due time so that the relationship ends only after the prescribed notice period expires. This obligation applies to both sides and is a central principle of German labor law.
messages.questions.288.study.note: The founding of Israel in 1948 as a Jewish state stands in immediate historical connection to the Holocaust—the genocide of six million Jews organized by Germany. This injustice commits the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany) permanently to special support for Israel's existence and security.
messages.questions.289.study.note: If someone is rejected solely because of their skin color, an outward characteristic decides instead of aptitude or performance—that is the essence of racist discrimination. The Allgemeines Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (General Equal Treatment Act) explicitly protects applicants in Germany from such irrelevant grounds for rejection in professional life.
messages.questions.290.study.note: Anyone who buys defective goods in Germany has a legal claim for remedy from the seller—regardless of whether the defect is only discovered at home. The buyer contacts the retailer directly and asserts the defect.
messages.questions.291.study.note: In the tax return, religious affiliation is asked because the state collects church tax on behalf of religious communities and must know who is a member. Since church tax is calculated as a surcharge on income or wage tax, declaring one's confession is a tax law requirement.
messages.questions.292.study.note: Religious tolerance in practice means that no one must justify or abandon their faith—each person decides for themselves what they believe or do not believe. This individual freedom of belief applies to everyone, regardless of origin or religious affiliation.
messages.questions.295.study.note: Church architecture, public holidays, marriage and family law, and the education system in Europe still bear clear traces of Christian tradition. In Germany, this influence is reflected, for example, in legal holidays such as Christmas and Easter or in the historical role of churches in the school system.
messages.questions.296.study.note: The term 'Advent' comes from Latin and means 'arrival'—it refers to the arrival of Christ. In German usage, 'Advent season' designates these four weeks, regardless of whether one observes them religiously or culturally.
messages.questions.297.study.note: From the 1960s onwards, under the German-Turkish recruitment agreement, large numbers of workers from Turkey came to Germany, leading to the formation of the country's largest immigrant group to this day.
messages.questions.298.study.note: The DDR (German Democratic Republic) brought workers from socialist allied countries into the country, including Vietnam, Poland, and Mozambique—these so-called contract workers filled gaps in industry, but mostly lived in segregated dormitories with little contact with the local population.
messages.questions.299.study.note: 'Guest worker' reflects the political expectation at that time that recruited workers would return to their home countries after temporary employment. In reality, many of them became a permanent part of German society.
messages.questions.300.study.note: The Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany) concluded its first recruitment agreement in 1955 with Italy, before agreements with other Southern European and later Mediterranean countries. Italy was thus the source country of the first organized labor migration to the Bundesrepublik in the postwar period.
messages.questions.304.study.note: In Berlin, elections to the Bezirksverordnetenversammlung (district assembly) have a minimum voting age of 16, which enables young people to participate early in democratic decision-making at the municipal level. This lowered voting age exceeds the 18-year minimum age that applies to Bundestag (federal parliament) elections and reflects the decision of the Berlin legislature to promote political participation before the age of majority.
messages.questions.306.study.note: In Berlin, the Landeszentrale für politische Bildung (state center for civic education) is the central contact point for anyone seeking factual, non-partisan information on political topics. It serves the entire population and is not committed to any particular political direction.
messages.questions.308.study.note: Berlin is both a city and a Bundesland (state) — one of three German city-states alongside Hamburg and Bremen. As a city-state, Berlin has a dual function: it is both the federal capital and an independent state with its own constitution and state government.
messages.questions.309.study.note: As a city-state, Berlin holds a special status within the German federal system, which is why the title of the head of government combines municipal and state functions in a single office.
messages.questions.310.study.note: Foreign relations are exclusively a federal matter in Germany, which is why there is no state-level Senate for this area. Berlin as a state may not conduct independent foreign relations — this responsibility lies solely with the federal government.
messages.glossary.5%-Hürde.context: The 5% threshold is a statutory ballot barrier in German electoral law: a party must receive at least 5 percent of second votes in the Bundestag (Federal Parliament) election to enter the Bundestag. This regulation is meant to prevent the parliament from fragmenting into many small factions. It thus serves political stability and facilitates the formation of governments capable of action.
messages.glossary.Abendgymnasium.context: An Abendgymnasium is a state-recognized school of the second educational pathway where adults can earn their Abitur (university entrance certificate) alongside work or family. It is aimed at people who did not complete their schooling at the usual age. Classes take place in the evening and thus put into practice Germany's anchored right to education and lifelong learning.
messages.glossary.Abgeordneter.context: A delegate (Abgeordneter) is a member of parliament legitimized by the people through universal, free, equal, and secret elections who represents the entire people. According to Article 38 of the Grundgesetz (German Basic Law), Bundestag delegates are not bound by any orders or instructions from their party and are accountable only to their conscience. This free mandate secures the independence of the people's representation against party interests and is a core feature of the German parliamentary system.
messages.glossary.Abgeordneter des Europäischen P: Members of the European Parliament are directly elected by citizens of EU member states and represent their interests at the European level. They participate in European Union legislation and oversee the work of the European Commission. The European Parliament is thus the only directly elected body of the EU.
messages.glossary.Abitur.context: The Abitur is the highest general education school diploma in Germany and entitles the holder to study at universities and colleges. It is awarded after successful completion of the upper secondary level. In addition to the regular school route, the Abitur can also be earned through the second educational pathway—for example at an evening gymnasium—so that this qualification remains accessible to adults.
messages.glossary.Adolf Hitler.context: Adolf Hitler was the chairman of the National Socialist Party (NSDAP) and became Chancellor of Germany in January 1933. He established a totalitarian dictatorship in which all state power was concentrated in his person—the so-called Führerprinzip (leadership principle)—and thereby ended the democratic order of the Weimar Republic. Under his rule, basic rights were abolished, political opponents were persecuted, and the Holocaust was systematically carried out.
messages.glossary.Adventszeit.context: Advent is the four weeks before December 25 and begins on the fourth Sunday before Christmas. It has Christian roots but has become a permanent part of social and cultural life in Germany. Typical customs include Advent wreaths, Advent calendars, and Christmas markets, which are practiced by people of different religious beliefs.
messages.glossary.AfD.context: The AfD (Alternative for Germany) is a right-populist party that has been represented in the Bundestag (German Federal Parliament) since 2013 and forms an independent parliamentary faction there. It advocates political positions that are rejected by most other parties in the Bundestag, which shapes its role in parliamentary operations.
messages.glossary.aktives Wahlrecht.context: Active voting rights (aktives Wahlrecht) is the right to cast a vote in an election. In Germany, all German citizens aged 18 and over have this right. It differs from passive voting rights, which refers to the right to stand as a candidate in an election.
messages.glossary.Alliierte Besatzungsmacht.conte: Following the unconditional surrender of Nazi Germany in May 1945, the four victorious powers of World War II—the USA, Soviet Union, Great Britain, and France—jointly assumed supreme governmental authority in Germany. They divided the country into four occupation zones, each administered by one of these powers. The Allied occupying powers thus formed, after the collapse of the Nazi state, the only legitimate state authority on German soil.
messages.glossary.Ampelkoalition.context: A traffic-light coalition (Ampelkoalition) is a governing alliance of the SPD, FDP, and Alliance 90/The Greens, whose party colors red, yellow, and green together form the colors of a traffic light. In Germany, it is customary to name coalitions after the colors of the participating parties to make the alliance recognizable at a glance. Such color designations are not official names but established political shorthand.
messages.glossary.Angebot und Nachfrage.context: In a market economy, supply and demand determine which goods are available in what quantities and at what prices. Businesses and consumers make their economic decisions largely freely, without central state planning. The state sets legal and social framework conditions to ensure fair competition and social balance.
messages.glossary.Anklage.context: A charge is the formal act by which the prosecutor brings a person before court for an alleged criminal offense. It initiates the actual criminal proceedings in which a court decides on guilt or innocence. Until a final judgment, the presumption of innocence applies—no one may be treated as guilty solely on the basis of a charge.
messages.glossary.Anspruch.context: A claim is a legally protected right of a person against the state, a government agency, or another body that can, if necessary, be enforced in court. It differs from a mere support opportunity or discretionary benefit in that the obligated party is required to fulfill it. Claims may arise from the Grundgesetz (German Constitution), from federal laws such as the Social Code, or from other legal regulations.
messages.glossary.Antisemitismus.context: Antisemitism refers to hostility, prejudice, and discrimination against Jewish women and men. It was a core ideological element of Nazism and led to the systematic mass murder of millions of Jewish people in Europe. In Germany, antisemitism is socially condemned; certain forms of expression, such as denying the Holocaust, are prohibited by law.
messages.glossary.Anwalt.context: A lawyer is a licensed legal professional who provides legal advice to citizens and represents them in court. In certain proceedings, such as a divorce before the family court, German procedural law requires mandatory legal representation, meaning at least one party must be represented by a lawyer. Lawyers are bound by confidentiality and professional ethics and form an important pillar of rule-of-law access to justice.
messages.glossary.Arbeitgeber.context: An employer is a person or company that employs others for compensation and has the authority to direct them. In the German social insurance system, employers are legally required to pay a set portion of their employees' contributions and remit them to the insurance carriers. German labor law also protects employees from arbitrary dismissals and binds employers to clear legal regulations.
messages.glossary.Arbeitnehmer.context: Employees are persons who work in a dependent employment relationship for an employer on the basis of an employment contract in exchange for compensation. As the structurally weaker contracting party, they enjoy special protection under German labor law, such as through dismissal protection, minimum wage regulations, and working time rules. For collective representation of interests against employers, they have access to trade unions and the instrument of collective agreements; furthermore, they pay a legally set portion of social insurance contributions that is deducted directly from their wages.
messages.glossary.Arbeitsgericht.context: The labor court is a specialized court for disputes between employees and employers, such as dismissals, wage claims, or collective labor law. It forms the first instance of the independent labor court system, above which stand the regional labor court and the federal labor court. This separate jurisdiction enables expert and expeditious resolution of labor law disputes.
messages.glossary.Arbeitsrechtsstreit.context: A labor law dispute is a legal disagreement between employees and employers over matters such as compensation, working conditions, or the legality of a dismissal. In Germany, there are separate labor courts that, as a specialized branch of the judiciary, ensure that both sides can enforce their claims equally before the court. This principle is part of the rule-of-law doctrine that all state powers are bound by law and every person has access to an independent court.
messages.glossary.Arbeitsverhältnis.context: An employment relationship is the legal relationship between an employee and an employer that is created by an employment contract. It establishes mutual rights and obligations, including wage payment, working hours, and dismissal procedures. German labor law sets legal minimum standards that protect employees from unsuitable working conditions.
messages.glossary.Asylrecht.context: The right to asylum is a fundamental right under Article 16a of the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) and grants protection in Germany to foreigners who are politically persecuted. It is the only fundamental right in the Grundgesetz that applies exclusively to non-Germans from the outset because it concerns protection from persecution by a foreign state. Its inclusion in the Grundgesetz is closely connected to experiences from the Nazi period, when many people depended on finding refuge abroad.
messages.glossary.Attentat.context: An assassination attempt is a targeted attack on a person, usually for political reasons. In German history, the assassination attempt of July 20, 1944 is particularly significant: resistance fighters tried to kill Hitler with a bomb to overthrow the Nazi dictatorship. The attempt failed; those involved were executed.
messages.glossary.Aufruf zur Vernichtung.context: Public calls for the destruction of the state of Israel are punishable in Germany and may be prosecuted as incitement to hatred under Section 130 of the German Criminal Code. The security of Israel is considered part of German reason of state, as Germany derives a special political and moral obligation from its responsibility for the Holocaust. This position reflects the lessons the German state has drawn from Nazi genocide policy.
messages.glossary.Aufstand.context: An uprising is a collective rising of the population against political or economic conditions, often linked with demands for freedom and justice. The popular uprising of 17 June 1953 in the DDR (German Democratic Republic) is the most famous example in post-war German history: workers rose up against state repression and demanded political participation. Such uprisings often end with violent suppression by the state when democratic paths to change are not open.
messages.glossary.Ausbildung.context: In Germany, vocational education (Ausbildung) refers above all to dual vocational training, in which young people acquire a recognized vocational qualification simultaneously at a workplace and at a vocational school. Such a qualification is considered an important foundation for access to skilled and well-paid positions. Since technological and economic change continuously transform the demands of the working world, lifelong learning alongside the initial qualification is becoming increasingly important.
messages.glossary.Ausländer.context: In legal terms, foreigners are all persons who do not hold German citizenship. The Grundgesetz (German Basic Law) distinguishes between rights belonging to everyone regardless of citizenship and rights reserved exclusively for German citizens. Asylum law holds a special position, as the Grundgesetz explicitly grants the right to asylum only to foreigners.
messages.glossary.Außenpolitik.context: Foreign policy comprises a state's relations with other countries and international organizations. In Germany, it is exclusively the responsibility of the Federation (Bund) — the 16 Länder (federal states) may not conduct independent foreign policy. This clear division of competence is anchored in the Grundgesetz and is an expression of German federalism (Föderalismus).
messages.glossary.Behinderung.context: Disability is explicitly named in Article 3, Section 3 of the Grundgesetz as a characteristic on account of which no one may be disadvantaged. This addition was incorporated into the Grundgesetz in 1994 and strengthens legal protection for people with disabilities against state and social exclusion. It is part of the general principle of equality that prohibits discrimination based on personal characteristics.
messages.glossary.Behörde.context: An authority (Behörde) is a state or municipal institution that carries out public tasks on behalf of the Federation, a Land (state), or a municipality and makes administrative decisions — such as tax offices, immigration authorities, or administrative offices. Under German administrative law, each authority may act only within its legally defined scope of responsibility and must comply with law and statute. Citizens have the right to contest administrative decisions using legally prescribed means, such as filing an objection (Widerspruch).
messages.glossary.Behördenleitung.context: The authority leadership (Behördenleitung) bears overall responsibility for the proper operation of a state authority and the conduct of its staff. In a Rechtsstaat (rule-of-law state), citizens have the right to lodge a complaint with the authority leadership if they feel they have been treated unfairly or poorly by a case officer. It is thus an important contact point for oversight of administrative action.
messages.glossary.Berliner Mauer.context: The Berlin Wall (Berliner Mauer) was erected by the DDR on 13 August 1961 to stop mass flight of the population to the west, and divided Berlin into an eastern and a western part for 28 years. It stood as a symbol of German division and the Cold War. On 9 November 1989 it fell as a result of the peaceful revolution in the DDR and paved the way for reunification (Wiedervereinigung).
messages.glossary.Berufsinformationszentrum.conte: The Career Information Centre (Berufsinformationszentrum, or BIZ) is an institution of the Bundesagentur für Arbeit (Federal Employment Agency) that provides free information on occupations, vocational training pathways, and the labour market. It is aimed at young people and adults who wish to obtain information about training and employment opportunities. The BIZ is part of the state infrastructure for promoting employment and vocational guidance.
messages.glossary.Besatzungsmacht.context: An occupying power (Besatzungsmacht) is a state that militarily controls the territory of a defeated country after a war and exercises sovereign rights there. After the end of the Second World War, the four allied victorious powers divided Germany into four occupation zones and jointly assumed supreme governmental authority. From this division eventually emerged two German states: the Federal Republic of Germany (Bundesrepublik Deutschland) in the west and the German Democratic Republic (Deutsche Demokratische Republik) in the east.
messages.glossary.Besatzungszone.context: After Germany's unconditional surrender in May 1945, the four victorious powers — the USA, Great Britain, France, and the Soviet Union — divided the country into four occupation zones, in each of which that power exercised supreme governmental authority. Since each zone was administered according to the political principles of the respective occupying power, the zones developed very differently. From this division eventually emerged two German states: the Federal Republic of Germany (Bundesrepublik Deutschland) in the west and the German Democratic Republic (Deutsche Demokratische Republik) in the east.
messages.glossary.Betriebsrat.context: The Betriebsrat (works council) is an employee-elected representation of interests within a company. It participates in social, personnel, and economic decisions of the employer on the basis of the Betriebsverfassungsgesetz (Works Constitution Act). This right of codetermination is meant to protect the rights of employees in the workplace and ensure a balanced relationship between employer and workforce.
messages.glossary.Bezirk.context: A Bezirk (district) is an administrative unit within a city-state like Berlin, which is simultaneously a Bundesland (federal state) and is divided into twelve such districts. Each district carries out independent administrative tasks and is led by an elected Bezirksverordnetenversammlung (district representatives' assembly) and a Bezirksamt (district office). The districts do not have their own parliament.
messages.glossary.Bezirksverordnetenversammlung.c: The Bezirksverordnetenversammlung (district representatives' assembly, BVV) is the elected parliament at the level of a Berlin Bezirk (district). Berlin is divided into twelve districts, and each BVV supervises the associated district administration and participates in district decisions. Within Berlin's urban structure, it thus performs a similar function to a municipal parliament in other German cities.
messages.glossary.Brandenburg.context: Brandenburg is one of the five Bundesländer (federal states) newly established in 1990 after reunification on the territory of the former DDR (East Germany). It lies in northeastern Germany and geographically surrounds the capital Berlin, but constitutes an independent Bundesland. As part of the federal order of the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany), Brandenburg has its own state parliament and its own responsibilities.
messages.glossary.Brauch.context: A custom is an inherited action or practice that is regularly repeated in a community and thus creates cultural identity. Customs are often connected to religious or regional particularities and are passed down from generation to generation. They belong to the lived everyday life of society and reflect shared values and ways of living.
messages.glossary.Briefgeheimnis.context: The right to privacy of postal communications (Briefgeheimnis) is anchored in Article 10 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects the confidentiality of written communication from interference by the state and by private parties. It is one of the classic freedom rights that secure the privacy of the individual. Opening someone else's letter without authorization violates this basic right and is also a criminal offense under the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code).
messages.glossary.Briefwahl.context: In Briefwahl (postal voting), eligible voters cast their ballot in writing without visiting the polling station: they request official voting materials, fill out the ballot, and send it back to the responsible authority within the deadline. It is a legally equivalent alternative to voting in person and applies to Bundestag (Federal Parliament), state parliament, and municipal elections. Postal voting enables political participation regardless of presence on election day.
messages.glossary.Bund.context: The Bund (Federation) refers to the national level of the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany), which exists alongside the 16 Bundesländer (federal states). Matters of general importance—such as foreign policy, defense, and federal legislation—fall within its responsibilities. The constitutional bodies of the Bund, including the Bundestag (Federal Parliament), Bundesrat (Federal Council), and Bundesregierung (Federal Government), act within the framework of the Grundgesetz for all citizens.
messages.glossary.Bundesadler.context: The Bundesadler (federal eagle) is the central state symbol of the Bundesrepublik and looks back on a centuries-old tradition of the eagle as a sign of authority and statehood in the German-speaking world. It appears both in the federal coat of arms and—in a more streamlined representation—in the plenary hall of the Bundestag, where it embodies the state sovereignty of Parliament.
messages.glossary.Bundesagentur für Arbeit.contex: The Bundesagentur für Arbeit (Federal Employment Agency) is a federal public body based in Nuremberg. It supports people in transitioning between jobs by placing them in employment and apprenticeships, paying unemployment benefits, and providing vocational guidance. As part of the German welfare state, it contributes to social protection in cases of unemployment.
messages.glossary.Bundesflagge.context: The Bundesflagge (federal flag) is the official state symbol of the Bundesrepublik and displays three horizontal stripes in black, red, and gold. These colors derive from the German freedom movement of the 19th century and were established as national colors in 1949 with the Grundgesetz. They stand for the democratic values and the state unity of Germany.
messages.glossary.Bundeskabinett.context: The Bundeskabinett (federal cabinet) consists of the federal chancellor (Bundeskanzler/Bundeskanzlerin) and all federal ministers and thus forms the Bundesregierung (Federal Government) as a whole. The ministers are formally appointed by the Bundespräsident (Federal President) on the proposal of the chancellor. As the central executive organ, the cabinet jointly makes the essential political decisions at the federal level.
messages.glossary.Bundeskanzleramt.context: The Bundeskanzleramt (Federal Chancellery) is the official seat and workplace of the Bundeskanzler in Berlin. It coordinates the work of the Bundesministerien (Federal Ministries) and is the organizational center of the Bundesregierung (Federal Government). As part of the executive, it contributes to implementing the government's political decisions and ensuring coordination among the individual departments.
messages.glossary.Bundespolitik.context: Federal politics encompasses the political decisions and legislative procedures at the level of the German state as a whole. In Germany's federal system, the Bund and the Länder (states) are closely interwoven: the Bundesrat (Federal Council) gives the Länder a direct voice in federal legislation. Through this, the majority relationships at state level directly affect the political options available to the federal government.
messages.glossary.Bundesrepublik Deutschland.cont: The Federal Republic of Germany is the official name of the democratic state established in 1949 in the territory of the western occupation zones, whose constitution is the Grundgesetz. The name combines the form of state (republic) with the federal structure: the Bund, Länder, and municipalities share state power. With reunification in 1990, the DDR (German Democratic Republic) joined this Federal Republic, which has comprised all 16 Bundesländer since then.
messages.glossary.Bundesstaat.context: Germany is a Bundesstaat (Federal State) in which state power is divided between the Bund and 16 Länder (states)—this principle is called federalism. Each Bundesland has its own parliament and its own government with specific responsibilities. Through the Bundesrat, the Länder participate in legislation at the federal level.
messages.glossary.Bundestagspräsident.context: The Bundestagspräsident (President of the Federal Parliament) directs the plenary sessions of the Bundestag and represents parliament to the outside world. The office is considered the second-highest state office of the Federal Republic of Germany, after the Bundespräsident. By parliamentary tradition, it is assumed by a member of the largest faction in the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.Bundestagssitz.context: The Bundestagssitz (Seat of the Federal Parliament) is the location where the German parliament, the Bundestag, convenes and performs its duties as the supreme legislative body. Since 1999, the Bundestag has met in the Reichstag building in Berlin, to which it moved from Bonn in the course of German reunification. This move was also a symbolic step in the return of capital functions to Berlin.
messages.glossary.Bundestagswahl.context: A Bundestag election is the election to the German Bundestag (Federal Parliament), the central legislative body of the Federal Republic of Germany, which takes place regularly every four years. The electoral principles – universal, direct, free, equal, and secret – are enshrined in Article 38 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) and realize the principle of popular sovereignty. The active right to vote belongs to all German citizens from age 18.
messages.glossary.Bundesverdienstkreuz.context: The Order of Merit of the Federal Republic of Germany is the highest state decoration of the Federal Republic of Germany for services by civilians. It belongs to the Order of Merit of the Federal Republic of Germany, which was founded in 1951, and is awarded by the Federal President. Recognized are special achievements in political, economic, cultural, intellectual, or social fields.
messages.glossary.Bundesverfassungsgericht.contex: The Federal Constitutional Court is the highest court of the Federal Republic of Germany and ensures that laws, government measures, and actions by constitutional bodies are compatible with the Basic Law. Citizens can petition it with a constitutional complaint if they believe their fundamental rights have been violated. As the only body, it may also impose a ban on a party if that party actively opposes the free democratic basic order.
messages.glossary.Bundesversammlung.context: The Federal Assembly is a constitutional body that convenes exclusively to elect the Federal President. It consists of all members of the Bundestag and an equal number of delegates sent by the parliaments of the 16 federal states. This procedure reflects Germany's federal structure by involving both the federal government and the states in the election of the head of state.
messages.glossary.Bundeswahlordnung.context: The Federal Election Ordinance (BWO) is a legal regulation that supplements the Federal Electoral Law and establishes the practical implementation rules for Bundestag elections. It regulates, among other things, the design and validity of ballot papers as well as organizational procedures surrounding the election. As a sub-legislative legal instrument, it specifies the legal requirements without being a formal law itself.
messages.glossary.Bundeswehr.context: The Bundeswehr is the armed forces of the Federal Republic of Germany and was founded in 1955. It operates under the primacy of politics: in peacetime it is subject to parliamentary control by the Bundestag, in case of defence it comes under the command of the Federal Chancellor. The principle of the 'citizen in uniform' ensures that soldiers retain their fundamental rights as responsible citizens.
messages.glossary.Bündnis 90/Die Grünen.context: Alliance 90/The Greens is a German party that emerged in 1993 from the merger of the West German Greens and the East German Alliance 90. It advocates especially for environmental and climate policy as well as for civil rights issues. The party is one of those represented in the German Bundestag and belongs to the democratic party spectrum of the Federal Republic.
messages.glossary.Bürgerinitiative.context: A citizens' initiative is a voluntary association of citizens who jointly represent a concrete political or social concern. It enables democratic participation outside of parties and elections, for instance through petitions, signature campaigns, or public actions. Citizens' initiatives are a recognized means of civil society participation at the municipal, state, and federal level.
messages.glossary.Bürgermeister.context: A mayor is the elected head of a municipality and directs the local administration. He or she represents the municipality to the outside world and implements the decisions of the municipal council. The exact duties and powers are set out in the municipal ordinances of the respective federal states, which is why they can vary slightly from state to state.
messages.glossary.Bürgerversammlung.context: A citizens' assembly is a form of direct democracy at the municipal level in which residents consult together on local matters. It is one of the means of political participation below the parliamentary level but has no independent state decision-making power. Unlike the constitutional bodies of the federal government, it is not anchored in the Basic Law.
messages.glossary.CDU.context: The CDU (Christian Democratic Union) is one of Germany's major people's parties and is located in the center-right conservative political spectrum. Since the founding of the Federal Republic in 1949, it has been a central actor in political will formation and has repeatedly participated in the federal government. In Bavaria, the CSU assumes a comparable role as an independent sister party; in all other federal states, the CDU is active.
messages.glossary.CDU/CSU.context: The CDU (Christian Democratic Union) and the CSU (Christian Social Union) are two separate parties that appear in the Bundestag as a joint parliamentary group – the Union faction. The CDU runs candidates in all federal states except Bavaria, while the CSU runs exclusively in Bavaria. Both parties rank among the major political forces in the German party system and stand for Christian democratic values.
messages.glossary.Christentum.context: Christianity is one of the world's most widespread religions and has fundamentally shaped law, culture, and social values in Europe over centuries. In Germany, both major denominations – Catholicism and Protestantism – influenced education, the calendar, and the foundations of the legal system. The freedom of faith and religion in the Basic Law today protects the right of all people to belong to a religion or to none.
messages.glossary.CSU.context: The Christian Social Union (CSU) is a political party that runs exclusively in Bavaria and is therefore the only large German party limited to a single state. It is positioned in the center-right political spectrum and is considered the sister party of the CDU, with which it cooperates closely at the federal level. Both parties traditionally form a joint parliamentary faction in the Bundestag (German parliament).
messages.glossary.Demonstration.context: A demonstration is a public assembly in which people collectively express their opinion or political demands. The Grundgesetz (German Constitution) protects this right in Article 8 as freedom of assembly. Public assemblies must be registered in advance with the competent authorities to ensure public safety and order.
messages.glossary.Deutsche Demokratische Republik: The German Democratic Republic (DDR, or East Germany) was the socialist state on the territory of the Soviet occupation zone, founded in 1949 and existing until 1990. As a one-party state under the leadership of the SED, fundamental rights such as freedom of opinion and travel were severely restricted. With reunification, the DDR joined the Federal Republic of Germany on October 3, 1990, and ceased to exist as an independent state.
messages.glossary.Deutsche Einheit.context: German Unity refers to the reunification of the Federal Republic of Germany and the German Democratic Republic on October 3, 1990. It was preceded by the peaceful revolution in the DDR, the opening of the Berlin Wall in 1989, and the Two Plus Four Treaty, which established the international political conditions for reunification. October 3 has since been the national holiday of the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.Diktatur.context: A dictatorship is a form of rule in which a person or group exercises all political power without democratic control, free elections, or adherence to fundamental rights. Separation of powers, freedom of opinion, and political opposition are either abolished or severely restricted. Germany experienced two dictatorships in the 20th century—the Nazi regime (1933–1945) and SED rule in the DDR—whose experiences fundamentally shaped the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) of 1949.
messages.glossary.Diskriminierung.context: Discrimination refers to the unjustified disadvantage of a person based on personal characteristics such as origin, skin color, gender, or disability. The Grundgesetz (German Constitution), Article 3(3), explicitly prohibits such unequal treatment, and the General Equal Treatment Act (AGG) specifies this protection in employment. This ensures that irrelevant personal characteristics cannot be used as grounds for disadvantage in hiring or in other areas of life.
messages.glossary.Drittes Reich.context: The Third Reich refers to the Nazi dictatorship in Germany from 1933 to 1945 under Adolf Hitler. The Weimar Republic was replaced by a totalitarian system based on racism, antisemitism, and the leadership principle, ending with the Holocaust of millions of people and military defeat in the Second World War. The experiences of this violent dictatorship fundamentally shaped the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) and the democratic order of the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.Ehe.context: Marriage in Germany is a legally recognized bond between two people, established through a civil ceremony. The Civil Code stipulates that an existing marriage must first be dissolved by divorce or death before a new one can be entered. Being married to more than one person at the same time—bigamy or polygamy—is a criminal offense under German law.
messages.glossary.Ehescheidung.context: Divorce refers to the judicial dissolution of a marriage. In Germany, only a family court has jurisdiction, and legal representation of at least one party is legally required. Divorce law governs matters such as spousal support, division of assets, and custody rights for joint children.
messages.glossary.Ehrenamtlicher Richter.context: A lay judge is a citizen without legal professional training who participates unpaid in court proceedings. This institution anchors democratic participation of the population in the administration of justice and strengthens trust in the judiciary. In the German legal system, lay judges are deployed in, among others, criminal, labour, and administrative courts.
messages.glossary.Einkommensteuer.context: Income tax is a direct tax that natural persons in Germany pay on their income. It is collected by the state and finances public tasks such as infrastructure, education, and social security. For taxpayers who are church members, it also serves as the basis for calculating church tax, which is assessed as a percentage surcharge on income tax.
messages.glossary.Einschulung.context: School enrollment marks the beginning of compulsory schooling, which in Germany typically begins at age six and means entry into primary school (Grundschule). Since education in Germany is a matter for the Länder, the individual federal states (Bundesländer) independently regulate the exact conditions and dates for school enrollment. With school entry, the legal entitlement to a place in a childcare facility also ends.
messages.glossary.Einspruch.context: An objection is a legal remedy that allows citizens to challenge an administrative decision – for example, a tax assessment. It must be filed on time and in writing with the responsible authority and initiates an administrative review procedure. If no objection is filed, the decision becomes final; only after an unsuccessful objection procedure does the path to court become available.
messages.glossary.Einwohnermeldeamt.context: The local registration office is a municipal authority where all persons living in Germany must register their residence. This legal registration requirement requires persons to re-register within two weeks of moving to a new place of residence. The registration forms the basis for numerous administrative services and enables the state to record the residence of the population with legal certainty.
messages.glossary.Eiserner Vorhang.context: The Iron Curtain referred to the political, ideological, and physical border that divided Europe after World War II into a democratic West and a socialist East under Soviet influence. This division shaped the living conditions of people on both sides for decades and ended with the peaceful revolution of 1989/1990.
messages.glossary.Elsass-Lothringen.context: Alsace-Lorraine is a region in northeastern France that was annexed to the German Empire following the Franco-Prussian War in 1871. After World War I, it returned to France in 1918 and does not belong to the Federal Republic of Germany today.
messages.glossary.Elterliches Erziehungsrecht.con: The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) guarantees parents in Article 6 the primary right to care for and educate their children. The state intervenes in this right only if parents cannot fulfil their educational responsibility or if the child's welfare is endangered. In doing so, the Grundgesetz follows the principle of subsidiarity: state action supplements, but does not replace, parental responsibility.
messages.glossary.Elterngeld.context: Parental allowance is a state social benefit that provides financial security to parents after the birth of a child so they can dedicate themselves to childcare. It is part of the German welfare state principle and must be actively requested from the responsible authority — it is not paid automatically.
messages.glossary.Elterngeldstelle.context: The parental allowance office is a state authority that receives, reviews, and approves applications for parental allowance. It is typically located in the welfare or social services offices of the Länder (federal states), as the implementation of the Federal Parental Allowance Act is the responsibility of the states. Parents contact this office after the birth of a child to apply for the income replacement benefit.
messages.glossary.Entlassung.context: A dismissal is the unilateral termination of an employment relationship by the employer. German labour law protects certain groups of people from unjustified dismissals, such as pregnant women and mothers through the Maternity Protection Act (Mutterschutzgesetz). Such protective regulations ensure the economic and social stability of particularly vulnerable employees.
messages.glossary.Ernennungsurkunde.context: The certificate of appointment is the official state document with which the Bundespräsident (Federal President) formally appoints high-ranking officials – including the Bundeskanzler (Chancellor) – to their office. It makes the assumption of office legally effective and is an expression of the representation duties of the Bundespräsident as regulated in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). The issuance of the certificate is a constitutive act in the constitutional procedure for government formation.
messages.glossary.Erwerbstätiger.context: A person is considered employed if they work in exchange for payment, whether as a dependent employee at a company or government agency, or as self-employed. Dependent employment is by far the most common form in Germany, in which employees have a contractual relationship with an employer and are subject to mandatory social insurance contributions. Employment status is significant both legally and for the welfare state, as it determines access to benefits such as pension, health, and unemployment insurance.
messages.glossary.Erziehungsberechtigter.context: A person with parental authority is someone to whom the legal right to care for a child belongs—usually parents, but also a guardian or foster parent. The right to raise children is anchored in the Basic Law (Art. 6 GG) as a fundamental right and protects the family from state interference in parenting decisions. The state assumes a supervisory role but only intervenes if the child's welfare is endangered.
messages.glossary.Euro.context: The Euro is the common currency of the European Union and replaced the Deutsche Mark as Germany's official means of payment on January 1, 2002. Its introduction was a central step in European economic integration, by which member states linked their economies more closely together. Today the Euro is the legal currency in the majority of EU states.
messages.glossary.Europäische Integration.context: European integration describes the gradual process by which European states have built common political, economic, and legal structures. Following the destruction of the two world wars, securing permanent peace was the primary focus, supplemented by the goal of shared prosperity. The result of this process is the European Union, which offers its members, among other things, a common internal market, a common currency, and freedom of movement.
messages.glossary.Europäische Union.context: The European Union is a political and economic union of European states that has been gradually built since the 1950s to permanently secure peace, stability, and prosperity after the world wars. Germany was one of the founding states in 1957 and remains closely linked to the EU's institutions and treaties. The union establishes common rules for trade, freedom of movement, and other policy areas and grants citizens concrete rights in their daily lives.
messages.glossary.Europäische Wirtschaftsgemeinsc: The European Economic Community (EEC) was founded in 1957 by the Treaty of Rome by six European states to create a common internal market with free movement of goods, people, services, and capital. Behind this lay not only an economic goal, but also the political conviction that close cooperation can secure lasting peace in Europe. Through further treaties, the EEC developed into the European Union as it exists today.
messages.glossary.Europäisches Parlament.context: The European Parliament is the only organ of the European Union that is directly elected by eligible citizens of the member states. It shares legislative authority with the Council of the EU and together with it decides the Union budget. Its official seat is in Strasbourg, chosen as a symbol of Franco-German reconciliation after the Second World War; additional workplaces are Brussels and Luxembourg.
messages.glossary.Europawahl.context: In European Parliament elections, citizens of EU member states directly elect the members of the European Parliament every five years. It is the only directly democratic election at the European level, in which the population of the entire Union determines its political representation. The European Parliament, as an elected body, participates in European legislation and represents the interests of EU citizens vis-à-vis other EU institutions.
messages.glossary.Exekutive.context: The executive is the executive power in the state and includes the government, administration, and police. It implements the laws passed by Parliament in practice and ensures their compliance. In the principle of separation of powers, it stands alongside the legislature, which passes laws, and the judiciary, which administers justice.
messages.glossary.falsche Behauptung.context: A false statement is an untrue factual claim about a person that can damage their reputation or standing. German criminal law protects people from such statements: deliberately or recklessly spreading untrue facts about others is punishable as defamation or slander. This protection principle derives from the right to personal honor and human dignity anchored in the Basic Law.
messages.glossary.Familie.context: Family is under the special protection of the Basic Law (Art. 6 GG), which guarantees parents the primary right to raise their children. This protection applies to various forms of living together and protects the family community from state intervention. However, where the child's welfare is seriously endangered, the state is both authorized and obligated to intervene.
messages.glossary.FDP.context: The Free Democratic Party (FDP) is a liberal party in Germany that emphasizes economic freedom, civil rights, and strong personal responsibility. It is one of the established parties in the German party system and is represented in the Bundestag. Its party color is yellow.
messages.glossary.Feiertag.context: Statutory public holidays are state-recognized rest days on which employees generally do not have to work. In Germany, many of these holidays have a Christian origin, as Christianity shaped the country's history and culture over centuries. Church festivals like Christmas, Easter, or Whitsunday are thereby anchored in law as public holidays.
messages.glossary.Festanstellung.context: A permanent employment contract is an employment relationship subject to social security contributions, in which the employer automatically pays contributions to pension, health, unemployment, and long-term care insurance through payroll accounting. It differs from self-employed work or minor employment through comprehensive social protection and employment law security. Germany's social insurance system is based on the solidarity principle: employers and employees share the contributions.
messages.glossary.Föderalismus.context: Federalism is a constitutional principle of the Federal Republic of Germany, according to which state tasks are divided between the Bund (federal government) and the 16 Länder (federal states). Certain policy areas – such as education policy – fall under the independent jurisdiction of the Länder, without the Bund being able to directly pass laws there. This division protects regional diversity and prevents excessive centralization of state power.
messages.glossary.Freie Demokratische Partei.cont: The Freie Demokratische Partei (Free Democratic Party, FDP) is a liberal party in Germany founded in 1948. It advocates for positions centered on economic freedom, individual civil rights, and limited government intervention. The FDP is among the parties represented in the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.freie Wahl.context: In a free election, every eligible voter makes their decision without external pressure, intimidation, or coercion. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) establishes this principle in Article 38 for elections to the Bundestag, making it a binding feature of democratic voting. No one may face personal disadvantages or consequences for their voting decision.
messages.glossary.Freiheit der Wahl.context: The freedom of choice is a principle of democratic electoral law: every eligible voter decides without coercion, pressure, or external influence whether and whom they vote for. This principle protects autonomous political will formation and is considered an essential prerequisite for genuine democracy.
messages.glossary.Freiheitsstrafe.context: A Freiheitsstrafe (prison sentence) is a sanction imposed by a court in which the convicted person is deprived of personal liberty through imprisonment. It is the most severe form of punishment in German criminal law and is imposed for particularly serious offenses. The sentence length is determined according to the severity of the act and is prescribed by law in the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code) for each offense.
messages.glossary.Freizügigkeit.context: Freizügigkeit (freedom of movement) is a fundamental right enshrined in Article 11 of the Grundgesetz that guarantees every person the right to freely choose their residence and place of stay within Germany. This right protects personal life planning from state interference and enables one to move or settle without government authorization. As part of civil liberties, freedom of movement is among the foundations of self-determined life in a constitutional state.
messages.glossary.Fünfprozenthürde.context: The five-percent threshold is a legal barrier clause in German electoral law: only parties that receive at least five percent of the second votes enter the Bundestag. It dates back to the experiences of the Weimarer Republik, where the fragmentation of parliament into many small parties prevented stable government formation. The threshold is intended to ensure efficient majorities and protect the political stability of the parliamentary system.
messages.glossary.Gastarbeiter.context: Gastarbeiter (guest workers) was the term for foreign workers whom the Federal Republic of Germany recruited from the 1950s onward through bilateral recruitment agreements – including with Italy, Spain, Greece, and Turkey – to fill labor shortages during the post-war economic boom. The term implied a temporary stay, yet many of those recruited remained permanently in Germany. They shaped the country's social development and laid the foundation for today's immigration society.
messages.glossary.Gaststättenerlaubnis.context: A Gaststättenerlaubnis (catering license) is an administrative permit required in Germany for operating restaurants, cafés, bars, and similar establishments. It ensures that operators are personally reliable and meet the legal requirements for hygiene, safety, and order. Without this permit, such an operation is not legal.
messages.glossary.Geheimheit der Wahl.context: Ballot secrecy is a constitutional principle that ensures no one learns how a person has voted. This protection shields voters from pressure, intimidation, or disadvantage based on their vote. Voting booths and sealed ballot envelopes put this principle into practice in the voting procedure.
messages.glossary.Geldstrafe.context: A fine is a penalty in German criminal law in which convicted persons must pay a sum of money to the state. As a purely financial measure, it does not affect physical integrity or human dignity and thus accords with the constitutional rights of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). Corporal punishment, by contrast, would be incompatible with these constitutional rights and is constitutionally prohibited in Germany.
messages.glossary.Gemeinde.context: A municipality is the smallest political and administrative unit in Germany and forms the lowest level of the federal state structure. The Grundgesetz guarantees it the right to municipal self-governance—that is, it independently manages matters of local concern, such as land-use planning, local infrastructure, and public facilities. In the area of political participation, the municipality also takes on direct responsibilities, such as maintaining the voter roll and distributing voting notices.
messages.glossary.Gemeindeverwaltung.context: Municipal administration is public administration at the local level—in cities, municipalities, and counties. It implements state tasks directly on the ground, including resident registration, building permits, and social services. As the lowest level of the federal system, it is the authority that citizens encounter most often in their daily lives.
messages.glossary.Gerichtsverfahren.context: A court proceeding is the legally regulated process in which an independent court examines a legal dispute or charge and makes a binding decision. In a rule-of-law state, fundamental procedural principles apply, including the right to a fair hearing and the independence of judges from state directives. These guarantees protect all parties from arbitrary decisions and ensure equality before the law.
messages.glossary.Gesetzeseinhaltung.context: In a rule-of-law state, all persons and state bodies are equally bound by applicable laws. Compliance with these laws ensures orderly coexistence and protects the rights of all citizens. State bodies such as the police are tasked with intervening in cases of violations and thus maintaining the foundation of the rule of law.
messages.glossary.Gesetzgebung.context: Legislation is the process through which binding legal norms are created. In the German system, responsibility for laws is divided between the federal government and the Länder (states), with the Bundesrat (Federal Council) bringing the interests of the states to bear at the federal level. The Grundgesetz determines who may enact laws in which areas and which procedural steps must be followed.
messages.glossary.Gewaltenteilung.context: The separation of powers divides state power into three independent areas: the legislature (legislation), the executive (administration and government), and the judiciary (adjudication). This principle prevents the concentration of power in a single institution and is regarded as an essential feature of the rule of law in the Grundgesetz. It thus protects the freedom and constitutional rights of all people from arbitrary state action.
messages.glossary.Gewerkschaft.context: A trade union is an organization that represents the economic and social interests of workers against employers and the state. It negotiates collective agreements that set wages, working hours, and working conditions in binding terms. The right to form trade unions is constitutionally guaranteed in Germany as freedom of association under Article 9, Section 3 of the Grundgesetz.
messages.glossary.Ghetto.context: A ghetto was a sealed-off and heavily guarded city district where Nazi occupiers forcibly imprisoned Jewish people during World War II. The residents lived there under extreme crowding, hunger, and violence before being deported to extermination camps. The Warsaw Ghetto Uprising of 1943 stands as a symbol of armed resistance against this policy of annihilation.
messages.glossary.Glaubens- und Gewissensfreiheit: Article 4 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) guarantees everyone the right to freely choose, profess, and practice a religion or worldview. This fundamental right protects both religious convictions and non-religious conscience decisions, and applies to believers as well as people without religious affiliation. The state may not force anyone to hold a particular belief or disadvantage them because of their religious or ideological conviction.
messages.glossary.Gleichbehandlung.context: Equal treatment means that all people, regardless of origin, skin color, gender, or religion, enjoy the same legal protection and may not be disadvantaged. Article 3 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) anchors this principle as a fundamental right and prohibits discrimination by both the state and private actors. The Allgemeine Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (General Equal Treatment Act, AGG) supplements this protection in everyday life, for example in access to goods, services, and employment.
messages.glossary.Gleichbehandlungsgrundsatz.cont: The principle of equal treatment is anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and obligates the state to treat all people equally regardless of characteristics such as origin, gender, religion, or disability. It protects against arbitrary disadvantage by state authorities and extends into social and labor-law areas as well. In the Allgemeine Gleichbehandlungsgesetz (AGG), this principle has taken on concrete legal form.
messages.glossary.Gleichberechtigung.context: Equal rights means that all people have the same rights and opportunities regardless of their gender. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) explicitly anchors this principle and thus obligates both state and society to prevent disadvantage based on gender. In working life, this protects women, for example, against dismissal or mistreatment because of pregnancy or other gender-specific circumstances.
messages.glossary.Gleichheit der Wahl.context: Equal voting is a constitutional principle that ensures every vote cast has the same counting value. No voter may be favored or disadvantaged. This principle belongs to the electoral principles of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects democratic participation from unequal weighting of votes.
messages.glossary.Gliederung.context: Division refers to the organization of the state into various levels, each with its own responsibilities and powers. In Germany, this division is anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and follows the principle of federalism, which provides for the Bund (federal government), the Länder (states), and municipalities as independent political levels. This structure prevents concentration of power and secures democratic control at multiple levels.
messages.glossary.Grenze.context: A state border defines the spatial jurisdiction of a state and is the basis of its sovereignty. Germany's present-day borders were definitively fixed by international treaties after reunification. This ended the postwar order, and Germany permanently recognized all existing borders with its neighboring states.
messages.glossary.Grundrecht.context: Fundamental rights are rights anchored in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) that protect individuals from state interference and directly bind all state powers. Most fundamental rights apply to all people in Germany regardless of their citizenship; however, some so-called Deutschenrechte (rights reserved for Germans) apply only to German citizens. The core of fundamental rights is permanently secured by the eternity clause (Article 79, Section 3 of the Grundgesetz) and cannot be abolished even by a constitutional majority.
messages.glossary.Gründungsmitglied.context: A founding member is a state that co-founded an international organization from the beginning and participated in drafting its foundational treaties. The Federal Republic of Germany was among six states that signed the Treaties of Rome in 1957, thereby laying the foundation for today's European Union. This early membership was an expression of the political will to institutionally anchor lasting peace and cooperation in Europe after the Second World War.
messages.glossary.Hausordnung.context: House rules are a binding set of regulations for communal living in multi-family buildings. They establish duties and behavioral standards for all residents, for example concerning quiet hours, use of common areas, and cleanliness in the building. Legally, house rules are usually part of the rental agreement or provided as a separate agreement and apply equally to all persons in the building.
messages.glossary.Hitler.context: Adolf Hitler was the Führer (leader) of the National Socialist movement and ruled Germany from 1933 to 1945 as a dictator. Under his rule, democratic fundamental rights were abolished, millions of people were systematically persecuted and murdered, and Europe was plunged into catastrophe by the Second World War. The experiences of this dictatorship shape the German Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and the commitment to defensive democracy to this day.
messages.glossary.Holocaust.context: The Holocaust designates the systematic persecution and murder of approximately six million Jews and millions of other people by the Nazi regime. This crime is a central point of reference in German memory culture and in responsibility for democracy and human rights. In Germany, publicly denying or downplaying the Holocaust is punishable as Volksverhetzung (incitement to hatred) under § 130 of the Strafgesetzbuch (Criminal Code), because it contradicts historical truth and violates the dignity of the victims.
messages.glossary.Interessenverband.context: An interest group is an organization that unites the common concerns of a particular social group and represents them to politics, the economy, and the public. In the labor world, trade unions perform this function for employees, while employer associations represent the interests of businesses. Interest groups are a permanent part of civil society in a pluralistic democracy.
messages.glossary.Jude.context: Jews are members of the Jewish people, who experienced discrimination and persecution in Europe over centuries. Under National Socialism, Jews were systematically disenfranchised and murdered in the Holocaust. Since 1948, when the State of Israel was founded as a Jewish state, its security has been considered part of German Staatsräson (state interest).
messages.glossary.Judikative.context: The judiciary is the judicial power in the state and, alongside the legislature and executive, forms one of the three powers in the principle of separation of powers. In Germany, independent courts and judges exercise this power by applying laws to specific cases. In doing so, they also control the other state powers and thus protect the fundamental rights of citizens.
messages.glossary.jüdische Gemeinde.context: A Jewish community is an organized community of Jewish citizens that sustains religious, cultural, and social life. Jewish communities can be documented in the territory of today's Germany since approximately the 4th century, making Jews one of the oldest population groups in the country. After their near-complete annihilation under National Socialism, Jewish communities in Germany were rebuilt after 1945 and grew again in the following decades through immigration.
messages.glossary.Jugendamt.context: The Jugendamt (youth office) is a municipal authority that advises families with parenting difficulties, offers support for parenting, and protects the wellbeing of children and young people. Its tasks are regulated in the Child and Youth Services Act (SGB VIII); its use is voluntary and confidential as long as the welfare of the child is not endangered. If the child's welfare is endangered—for example through abuse or neglect—the Jugendamt can intervene protectively within the framework of the state's guardian role under the Grundgesetz (Constitution).
messages.glossary.Kalter Krieg.context: The Cold War was a global power struggle between the Western democracies led by the USA and the Eastern Bloc led by the Soviet Union, which shaped international politics from 1945 to 1991. Although there was no direct war between the superpowers, ideology, armament, and political influence were used as weapons. Germany was divided by this conflict: the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic of Germany) belonged to the Western alliance, and the DDR (German Democratic Republic) to the socialist Eastern Bloc.
messages.glossary.Kapitulation.context: Capitulation is the formal military submission of a state to its opponent. With the unconditional capitulation on May 8, 1945, Germany handed over its armed forces without reservation to the Allied powers and thus lost any say in the post-war order. It formed the legal foundation for the division of Germany into occupation zones and for the subsequent political reorganization of the country.
messages.glossary.Kindererziehung.context: The Grundgesetz (Constitution) establishes in Article 6 the care and education of children as the natural right of parents and assigns them primary responsibility for their children's upbringing. The state assumes a supplementary guardian role and may only intervene if the child's welfare is endangered. This principle reflects the relationship between familial self-responsibility and the state's protective duty.
messages.glossary.Kindergarten.context: Kindergarten is a state-recognized institution for early childhood education and care for children from age three until school entry. It is operated by municipalities, independent, or denominational providers and is part of the public education system. The offering supports child development and helps families balance family and work.
messages.glossary.Kirchensteuer.context: Church tax (Kirchensteuer) is a levy that tax-authorized religious communities are permitted to collect from their members. The state collects it through tax offices as a percentage surcharge on income tax or wage tax. Only members of such a community are required to pay; those who withdraw are exempt from the tax.
messages.glossary.Kniefall.context: On December 7, 1970, Chancellor Willy Brandt knelt at the memorial of the Warsaw Ghetto—a silent, unplanned gesture of sorrow and humility. This kneeling (Kniefall) has since stood as a symbol of Germany's willingness to acknowledge responsibility for the crimes of National Socialism and to confront the past.
