It is considered one of the most significant moments in German memory culture in relation to the victims of the Nazi dictatorship.
messages.glossary.Koalition.context: A coalition is an alliance of several parties that form a government together after an election and jointly carry out its policy. It arises because in German parliamentarism, no single party regularly achieves an absolute majority of seats. The parties involved set out their joint political objectives in a coalition agreement.
messages.glossary.Kommunalwahl.context: In local elections, citizens elect the people's representatives at the local level, such as in municipalities, cities, or city districts. Since the rules for local elections are a matter for the Bundesländer (federal states), regulations such as the minimum age can vary from state to state.
messages.glossary.Kommune.context: Municipalities—cities, towns, and counties—form the lowest level of state organization in Germany. They are responsible for many tasks of daily life, including kindergartens, local infrastructure, and the registration system. The Grundgesetz (German Constitution) guarantees municipalities the right to self-governance, so they can regulate their local affairs independently.
messages.glossary.Krankenversicherung.context: Health insurance is a central part of the German social insurance system and ensures access to medical care for all insured persons. In Germany, most working people are subject to compulsory statutory insurance, with contributions shared by employees and employers. This principle of solidarity ensures that health services can be used regardless of individual income.
messages.glossary.Kündigung.context: A dismissal is a unilateral declaration to end an employment relationship—it can be made by an employer or by an employee. In Germany, the Protection Against Wrongful Dismissal Act protects employees from arbitrary job loss by requiring that a dismissal must be objectively justified. If a dismissal violates this or other statutory provisions, it is considered unjustified and can be challenged before the labor court.
messages.glossary.Kündigungsfrist.context: A notice period is the legally or contractually prescribed period of time that must pass between the dismissal of an employment relationship and its actual end. It protects both employees and employers from an abrupt termination of the employment relationship and gives both sides time to adjust to the new situation. Under German labor law, the statutory minimum notice period for employees is four weeks; for employers, longer periods apply depending on the duration of employment.
messages.glossary.Kündigungsschutzklage.context: A wrongful dismissal suit is a labor law remedy by which employees can have a dismissal reviewed before a labor court to determine whether it is lawful. If the dismissal is declared invalid, the employment relationship continues. The suit must typically be filed within three weeks of receipt of the dismissal notice.
messages.glossary.Land.context: Germany is divided into 16 Bundesländer (federal states), each of which has its own parliament and government. The states are mainly responsible for areas such as education, police, and culture. Through the Bundesrat (Federal Council), they jointly influence the legislation of the federal government.
messages.glossary.Landesflagge.context: A state flag is the official emblem of a German Bundesland (federal state) and an expression of its federal independence within the Federal Republic. It differs from the federal flag and typically bears the historical colors or symbols of the respective state. In Germany's federal system, all 16 Bundesländer have their own emblems, including a flag and coat of arms.
messages.glossary.Landesparlament.context: A state parliament is the elected people's representative body of one of Germany's sixteen Bundesländer (federal states). It passes laws applicable in the respective state and supervises the state government. Depending on the state, it carries its own name—in Berlin it is called the Abgeordnetenhaus, in Bavaria the Landtag, and in Hamburg the Bürgerschaft.
messages.glossary.Landesregierung.context: Each of Germany's 16 Bundesländer (federal states) has its own state government, led by a minister-president. It is responsible for policy areas that the Grundgesetz (German Constitution) assigns to the states, including education, police, and culture. Federalism thus ensures independent political governance at the state level alongside the federal government.
messages.glossary.Landeszentrale für politische B: The State Centre for Civic Education is a state institution at the state level with the legal mandate to provide civic education to all citizens regardless of party affiliation or ideology. It provides information, materials, and events on political, historical, and social topics. In doing so, it contributes to strengthening democratic participation and promoting civic awareness among the population.
messages.glossary.Landtag.context: The Landtag is the elected parliament of a German Bundesland (state). It passes state laws, adopts the state budget, and elects the state's head of government. Each of the 16 Bundesländer has its own Landtag and thus an independent parliamentary representation.
messages.glossary.Landtagswahl.context: A Landtagswahl (state parliament election) is the election of a German Bundesland's parliament, the Landtag. Because Germany is a federal state with 16 Bundesländer, Landtagswahlen take place independently of one another and at different times. They follow the same electoral principles as Bundestag elections: universal, free, equal, secret, and direct.
messages.glossary.Lebensversicherung.context: Life insurance is a private, voluntarily obtained form of financial protection that provides coverage in case of death or upon reaching a certain age. It is not part of the statutory social insurance system because it is neither legally required nor based on the solidarity principle of mandatory insurance branches. Instead, it is based on an individual contract between the insured person and a private insurance company.
messages.glossary.Legislative.context: The legislature is the legislative branch of government and is responsible for enacting laws that are binding on all. In the German Grundgesetz (Basic Law), it is established as an independent branch and separated from the executive and judiciary—a principle intended to prevent abuse of power. At the federal level, the Bundestag and Bundesrat primarily exercise this function.
messages.glossary.Lehrstelle.context: An apprenticeship position is a training place in Germany's dual vocational education system, which combines on-the-job training with classroom instruction. Those seeking an apprenticeship position apply to a company for an apprenticeship contract, which forms the legal basis of the training relationship. The dual system is considered an essential part of German education and economic life.
messages.glossary.Lohnsteuer.context: Wage tax is the income tax for employees; the employer calculates it and submits it directly to the tax office. It is thus a special form of general income tax collection and facilitates tax administration. Church tax is linked to wage tax, which is why the employer must know the religious affiliation of employees.
messages.glossary.Luftbrücke.context: The Luftbrücke (Berlin Airlift) refers to the Western Allied supply flights to West Berlin, which were established from 1948 to 1949 in response to the Soviet blockade of all land and water routes. For months, food, coal, and other goods were flown exclusively by air into the blockaded city. The event is considered a defining symbol of the Cold War and Western resistance to Soviet pressure.
messages.glossary.Makkabi-Sportverein.context: Makkabi is an international Jewish sports movement that emerged in the late 19th century in the context of modern Zionism and combines sport with Jewish culture and community. In Germany, there are about 40 Makkabi sports clubs that continue this tradition and are part of organized civil society. Membership is in principle open to all people, regardless of origin or religion.
messages.glossary.Mehrheit.context: A majority means that more than half of the votes in a parliament or chamber fall on one side. In the Bundestag, it ensures that the government can act; in the Bundesrat, it determines whether states' interests can approve or block a federal law. Without the required majority, laws can fail or be delayed through mediation procedures.
messages.glossary.Meinung.context: An opinion is a value judgment or personal view protected by the freedom of opinion enshrined in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). Unlike factual claims, an opinion cannot be evaluated as true or false but rather reflects an individual or political conviction. The diversity of opinions in a society is considered the foundation of democratic order because it enables open competition of political ideas.
messages.glossary.Menschenwürde.context: Human dignity is established as inviolable in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and thus stands at the head of all fundamental rights. Unlike other fundamental rights, it cannot be restricted or balanced against other interests. This absolute protection principle was deliberately formulated in response to the systematic deprivation of rights and destruction of human beings under Nazi dictatorship.
messages.glossary.Merkmal.context: A characteristic is a distinguishing individual element that sets a concept, institution, or system apart from others. In civic and legal education, characteristics are used to describe and differentiate, for example, democratic orders, rule-of-law principles, or constitutional bodies precisely from one another. Recognizing key characteristics helps systematically understand political and social structures.
messages.glossary.Migrant.context: A migrant is a person who has relocated their permanent residence from another country to Germany. Migration has historically shaped German society—for instance, through state-organized recruitment of foreign workers from the 1960s onward, which led to the formation of permanent immigrant communities. Germany today understands itself as an immigration country in which migrants actively shape social, cultural, and economic life.
messages.glossary.Minister.context: Ministers each lead a federal ministry and are members of the Bundesregierung (federal government) together with the Bundeskanzler (federal chancellor). They are appointed by the Bundespräsident (federal president) on the proposal of the Bundeskanzler, who sets the political guidelines. As part of the executive branch, they are responsible for implementing laws and directing policy within their area of responsibility.
messages.glossary.Ministerium für Staatssicherhei: The Ministerium für Staatssicherheit (Ministry for State Security, Stasi) was the domestic intelligence agency of the DDR (East Germany) and one of the most extensive surveillance systems in history—it systematically gathered information about its own population and persecuted political dissidents. After the end of the DDR, Stasi files were transferred to a specially established federal authority that grants affected persons access to their own records.
messages.glossary.Ministerpräsident.context: The Ministerpräsident (Minister-President) leads the state government of a Bundesland (federal state) and is the highest executive office at the state level. In the city-states of Berlin, Hamburg, and Bremen, this office carries different titles: Regierender Bürgermeister (Governing Mayor), Erste Bürgermeisterin (First Mayor), or Bürgermeister (Mayor). Ministers-President represent their states in the Bundesrat (federal council) and thus participate in federal legislation.
messages.glossary.Monarchie.context: In a monarchy, state power rests with a monarch—a king, queen, or emperor—whose office is typically hereditary. Germany was an empire and thus a monarchy until 1918. Since then, the Grundgesetz (Basic Law, German constitution) establishes Germany as a republic in which the head of state is democratically determined.
messages.glossary.Montagsdemonstration.context: The Monday demonstrations (Montagsdemonstrationen) were regular, nonviolent street protests in the DDR (East Germany) that took place primarily in Leipzig starting in fall 1989. Participants demanded fundamental rights, freedom of movement, and democratic participation. They are considered a central part of the peaceful revolution that led to the fall of the Berlin Wall and German reunification.
messages.glossary.Mutterschutz.context: Maternity protection is a legally anchored safeguard for pregnant women and mothers shortly after childbirth, regulated by the Mutterschutzgesetz (Maternity Protection Act). It includes a prohibition on work in the weeks before and after delivery and a ban on dismissal that prevents pregnancy from being used as grounds for termination. The goal is to ensure the health and economic security of mother and child.
messages.glossary.Nachtruhe.context: Night quiet refers to the legally protected rest period that typically runs from 10 p.m. to 6 a.m. in Germany. During this time, noise disturbances—such as loud music, home repairs, or celebrations—must be avoided to protect neighbors' right to undisturbed sleep. The specific regulations are set out in state pollution control laws, municipal ordinances, and house rules.
messages.glossary.Nationalhymne.context: The national anthem is a state's official musical symbol and expression of its shared values. In Germany, the third stanza of the Deutschlandlied (Song of Germany) serves as the national anthem because its key terms—unity, justice, and freedom—correspond to the fundamental principles of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). This stanza was initially adopted for the Bundesrepublik (Federal Republic) and confirmed for all of Germany after reunification in 1990.
messages.glossary.Nationalsozialismus.context: National Socialism was a totalitarian ideology and form of rule in Germany from 1933 to 1945 based on racism, antisemitism, and the Führerprinzip (leadership principle). The regime completely eliminated democratic institutions, fundamental rights, and the rule of law, and systematically persecuted and murdered millions of people, including Jews, Sinti and Roma, and political opponents. The Holocaust and World War II are the gravest consequences of this dictatorship; remembrance of its victims is a fixed part of German democratic self-understanding.
messages.glossary.Nationalsozialist.context: National Socialists were followers of the NSDAP (Nazi Party) and its ideology based on racism, antisemitism, and the Führerprinzip (leadership principle). Under their rule, democratic fundamental rights were abolished and systematic persecution and extermination of people were carried out. The Nazi dictatorship ended in 1945 and serves as the benchmark for why the protection of fundamental rights and democracy in Germany today has constitutional status.
messages.glossary.Nationalsozialistisches Verbrec: National Socialism (1933–1945) was a dictatorship founded on racism, antisemitism, and terror that led to the systematic persecution and murder of millions of people—including approximately six million Jews in the Holocaust. These crimes against humanity form a central part of German memory culture and political responsibility. From this historical guilt, the Federal Republic of Germany derives lasting consequences for its domestic and foreign policy, such as the obligation to uphold human rights and special responsibility for Israel's security.
messages.glossary.NS-Staat.context: The NS state refers to Nazi Germany under Adolf Hitler's dictatorship from 1933 to 1945. During this period, democratic institutions were destroyed, fundamental rights were abolished, and the state was built on racist ideology, the leadership principle, and totalitarian control. The experiences of this dictatorship fundamentally shaped the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) of the Federal Republic of Germany: it was intended to ensure that such rule would never be possible again.
messages.glossary.öffentliche Verbreitung.context: Public dissemination refers to the communication of a statement or information to an unspecified, potentially unlimited group of people—such as through the press, radio, social media, or public speeches. In law, it is classified as particularly serious because it can directly affect reputation protection, personal rights, and public discourse. Therefore, legal norms, such as those protecting against defamation or establishing media responsibility, specifically attach consequences to public dissemination.
messages.glossary.Opposition.context: The opposition consists of the members of parliament who do not belong to the government coalition. Their task is to publicly monitor government action, criticize it, and present alternatives. A functioning opposition is considered an indispensable feature of parliamentary democracy.
messages.glossary.Ordnungsamt.context: The municipal order office is an agency of local government responsible for enforcing laws and regulations at the local level. It acts on behalf of the municipality and oversees, among other things, noise protection, parking regulations, and business supervision. In this way, it contributes to maintaining public order in the immediate living environment of the population.
messages.glossary.Ostvertrag.context: The Eastern Treaties (Ostverträge) are a series of bilateral agreements that the Federal Republic of Germany concluded in the early 1970s with the Soviet Union, Poland, the German Democratic Republic (DDR), and other East European states. They recognized the borders that emerged after World War II under international law and formed the foundation for political détente between Western and Eastern Europe. This policy of rapprochement, known as the New Eastern Policy (Neue Ostpolitik), fundamentally shaped German foreign policy during the Cold War era.
messages.glossary.Parlament.context: A parliament is the elected popular representation of a state; in Germany, the Bundestag (federal parliament) assumes this role at the federal level. It passes laws, elects the Federal Chancellor (Bundeskanzlerin or Bundeskanzler), and monitors the work of the Federal Government (Bundesregierung). Only parties that exceed the five-percent threshold or win at least three direct mandates in the election are represented in the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.parlamentarische Opposition.con: Parliamentary opposition refers to the factions in parliament that do not belong to the government. Their main task is to critically examine government action, develop alternatives, and publicly expose failures. A functioning opposition is considered an indispensable element of democratic separation of powers and oversight.
messages.glossary.Parlamentsmitglied.context: A member of parliament belongs to an elected representative body and participates in lawmaking and political decision-making. In Germany, parliaments exist at the federal, state, and local levels, with members receiving their mandate through free elections. They represent the interests of the population, monitor the government, and enact binding laws.
messages.glossary.Parteiverbot.context: The Basic Law (Grundgesetz) protects the multi-party system as an essential feature of the free democratic basic order—a lesson from the National Socialist dictatorship, which after 1933 banned all parties except the Nazi Party (NSDAP) and thereby destroyed democratic competition. Today, only the Federal Constitutional Court (Bundesverfassungsgericht) can impose a party ban, if a party aims to abolish the democratic basic order. This regulation is anchored in Article 21, Paragraph 2 of the Basic Law.
messages.glossary.Pflegeversicherung.context: Long-term care insurance (Pflegeversicherung) is the fifth pillar of German social insurance and was introduced in 1995. It provides coverage for people who permanently depend on external help—such as due to age or serious illness. Employees and employers share the contribution costs.
messages.glossary.Planwirtschaft.context: In a planned economy (Planwirtschaft), the state determines centrally what is produced, distributed, and at what price goods are sold. Private enterprises and free markets play hardly any role; instead, state plans control the entire economy. The DDR followed this system, which stands in contrast to the social market economy, in which supply and demand substantially determine prices and production decisions.
messages.glossary.Plenarsaal.context: The Plenarsaal is the central assembly hall of the Deutscher Bundestag (German Federal Parliament), where all elected members convene to debate and pass laws. It is located in the Reichstagsgebäude in Berlin and is the place where parliamentary debates take place publicly. As the heart of the legislature, it embodies the principle of popular sovereignty, since it is here that the elected representatives of the people form the political will.
messages.glossary.Polizei.context: The police are a state authority responsible for public security and order. In Germany, the police are primarily a matter for the Bundesländer (federal states): the 16 Bundesländer bear the main responsibility for organizing and deploying their respective police forces. Central tasks include hazard prevention, criminal prosecution, and the enforcement of existing laws.
messages.glossary.Presse.context: The press is an independent social institution that publicly observes, reports on, and comments on state actions. It is often called the 'fourth estate' because, alongside the legislature, executive, and judiciary, it exercises a controlling function in democracy. Press freedom is enshrined in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) and protects journalists from state interference in their reporting.
messages.glossary.Pressefreiheit.context: Press freedom is enshrined in Article 5 of the Grundgesetz and protects newspapers, magazines, and other media from state interference in their reporting. It is an essential condition for democratic opinion formation because independent media can publicly control state actions. As a fundamental right, it enjoys special constitutional protection that cannot be suspended even by a parliamentary majority.
messages.glossary.Pressezensur.context: Press censorship refers to state control or suppression of media content before it is published. The Grundgesetz explicitly forbids censorship and guarantees in Article 5 press freedom as a central fundamental right of the democratic order. A free press is thus considered an indispensable precondition for political opinion formation and the control of state power.
messages.glossary.Privatpolizei.context: A private police force is a non-state organization that assumes security service tasks, such as protection of objects or persons. In the German Rechtsstaat (constitutional state), the monopoly on force rests exclusively with the state, which is why private security services may not exercise sovereign powers such as arrest or coercive measures. Instead, they act within the framework of general private law and are subject to state supervision.
messages.glossary.Prozess.context: A trial is a formal judicial proceeding in which legal disputes between parties (civil trial) or criminal charges against a person (criminal trial) are conducted according to established legal rules. It is a core element of the Rechtsstaat because it ensures that conflicts are decided not by arbitrary means but by independent courts according to law.
messages.glossary.Rassismus.context: Racism is the belief that people have different worth based on their alleged membership in a 'race.' Under National Socialism, racism became state doctrine: laws and authorities carried out the systematic disenfranchisement, persecution, and destruction of Jews, Sinti and Roma, and other groups. The Grundgesetz of the Federal Republic forbids discrimination based on race and draws a direct consequence from this historical injustice.
messages.glossary.Rechtsanwalt.context: A lawyer is a licensed legal professional who advises private individuals and businesses in legal matters and represents them in court. The independent legal profession is an essential component of the Rechtsstaat because it ensures access to justice. In many German court proceedings there is compulsory legal representation, meaning parties must be represented by a lawyer.
messages.glossary.Rechtsfrage.context: A legal question arises when it is unclear how a statutory regulation applies to a concrete situation. In a Rechtsstaat, citizens have the right to have their interests represented and enforced in court through qualified legal advice. Lawyers are independent organs of the administration of justice who support the protection of rights and duties.
messages.glossary.rechtsprechende Gewalt.context: The judiciary is one of the three branches of state power in the German constitutional system, alongside the legislature and the executive. It is exercised by independent courts that decide whether laws, administrative decisions, and actions by private persons are compatible with existing law. The independence of the courts is a central feature of the Rechtsstaat and protects citizens from arbitrary interference by the state.
messages.glossary.Rechtsprechung.context: The administration of justice is the sovereign task of authoritatively deciding what is lawful in specific disputes. It constitutes one of the three powers in the German principle of separation of powers and rests exclusively with independent courts. This institutional independence protects individuals from government or parliament interfering in judicial decisions.
messages.glossary.Rechtsstaat.context: In the Rechtsstaat (constitutional state governed by the rule of law), everyone—citizens and state organs and authorities alike—is equally bound by law. No person and no institution stands above the law; this principle of legal bindingness protects against arbitrary state action and secures fundamental rights for all. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) anchors the Rechtsstaat as a foundational constitutional principle of the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.Regierender Bürgermeister.conte: The Regierender Bürgermeister (Governing Mayor) is the head of state and head of government of the state of Berlin and leads the Berlin Senate, the state government. Because Berlin is simultaneously a city municipality and a federal state (Bundesland), this office combines the function of a mayor with that of a minister-president in a single title. In the other federal states, the comparable office is called Ministerpräsident (Minister-President), or in the city states of Hamburg and Bremen, Erster Bürgermeister (First Mayor) or Bürgermeister (Mayor).
messages.glossary.Regierung.context: Government is the executive constitutional organ that steers state politics and implements enacted laws. In Germany, the formation of a federal government (Bundesregierung) presupposes a majority in the Bundestag (Federal Parliament), which typically requires a coalition agreement among several parties. Regular elections bind government to the will of the people and secure its democratic legitimacy.
messages.glossary.Regierungsbildung.context: Government formation is the process of establishing a government capable of governing following an election, based on a parliamentary majority. If many small parties are represented in parliament, the search for a stable majority coalition becomes more difficult. The five-percent threshold in German electoral law serves to prevent excessive parliamentary fragmentation and thereby facilitate government formation.
messages.glossary.Regierungspartei.context: A governing party is a party that participates in the formation of government and supports it in parliament. It provides ministers or supports the head of government through its parliamentary majority. Parties not involved in government assume the role of opposition and critically oversee government work.
messages.glossary.Regierungsvertreter.context: Government representatives are members of state governments (Landesregierungen) who represent their respective federal states (Bundesländer) in the Bundesrat (Federal Council). They are not elected by the people but delegated by state governments and are bound by their instructions. The number of votes a state holds in the Bundesrat depends on the state's population.
messages.glossary.Regierungswechsel.context: A change of government occurs when, after an election, a new political majority assumes government responsibility. At the federal level and in the states, the governing majority determines which political direction is pursued. Because state governments cast their votes in the Bundesrat (Federal Council), a change of government in one state also shifts the political balance in the Bundesrat.
messages.glossary.Reichskanzler.context: The Reichskanzler (Reich Chancellor) was the highest government office in the German Reich and managed government business similarly to how the Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor) does today. In the Weimar Republic, he was appointed by the Reichspräsident (Reich President) and was accountable to parliament. The appointment of Adolf Hitler as Reichskanzler in January 1933 marked the beginning of the Nazi seizure of power and the end of democratic order.
messages.glossary.Reklamation.context: A complaint is a formal objection to defective goods or services directed to the seller or provider. In Germany, the right to lodge a complaint is protected by statutory warranty provisions in the Civil Code (Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch, BGB). The seller is obligated to remedy the defect, provide a replacement, or refund the purchase price.
messages.glossary.Religionsgemeinschaft.context: A religious community is an organized association of people who share common religious beliefs, practices, and traditions. In Germany, the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) guarantees religious freedom, so religious communities can exist independently of the state and regulate their internal affairs autonomously. Under certain conditions, they can obtain the status of a public law body (Körperschaft des öffentlichen Rechts), which grants them special rights in relation to the state.
messages.glossary.Religionsunterricht.context: According to Article 7 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law), religious education is a regular subject in German public schools and is taught in accordance with the principles of the respective religious community. Until age 14, parents decide on their child's participation; after that, the child decides. This provision combines the state's educational mandate with the fundamental right to religious freedom.
messages.glossary.religiöse Toleranz.context: Religious tolerance designates mutual respect among people of different religious convictions and willingness to respect other religions and worldviews. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) anchors freedom of belief and conscience as a fundamental right that the state protects for all people—regardless of their religious affiliation or lack thereof. The separation of state and church thereby forms the institutional foundation ensuring that no religion in Germany is favored or disadvantaged.
messages.glossary.Republik.context: In a republic, state power originates from the people; there is no hereditary head of state. Elected representatives exercise power in the name of the population and are accountable to it. The Grundgesetz anchors the republican principle in Article 20 as an unchangeable constitutional principle.
messages.glossary.Resolution der Vereinten Nation: A United Nations resolution is a formal decision made by the UN General Assembly or the Security Council to take a position on international questions or to recommend concrete measures. Such resolutions form an important foundation of international law and can set the political direction for the international community. Resolution 181 of 1947, for example, determined the partition of the British-mandated territory of Palestine and thereby created the legal basis under international law for the founding of the state of Israel.
messages.glossary.Römischer Vertrag.context: The Treaties of Rome were signed in 1957 in Rome by six Western European states—including the Federal Republic of Germany—and created the legal foundation for the European Economic Community (EEC) and the European Atomic Energy Community (Euratom). They established common institutions and the framework for a European internal market with binding provisions governing the economic and legal cooperation of member states. The treaties are considered a decisive turning point for the later development into the European Union.
messages.glossary.Rosenmontag.context: Rosenmontag (Rose Monday) is the high point of Carnival and falls on the Monday before Ash Wednesday. In many German cities, especially in the Rhineland, large parades with costumes, music, and floats move through the streets on this day. Carnival has deep roots in German folk culture and is celebrated especially in Cologne, Düsseldorf, and Mainz as a significant regional festival.
messages.glossary.Sachsen.context: Saxony (Sachsen) is a state in eastern Germany and belonged to the territory of the DDR (German Democratic Republic) until 1990. After reunification, it was re-established as the Free State of Saxony, with Dresden as the state capital. As one of the 16 states, Saxony participates in federal legislation through the Bundesrat.
messages.glossary.Sachsen-Anhalt.context: Saxony-Anhalt (Sachsen-Anhalt) is one of five states founded on the territory of the former DDR (German Democratic Republic) after German reunification in 1990. It lies in central Germany and, like all states, is endowed with its own state competencies through federalism.
messages.glossary.Scheidung.context: A divorce is the judicial dissolution of a marriage by a family court. It is granted when the marriage is deemed to have failed—that is, when the marital living community no longer exists permanently and its restoration cannot be expected. Divorce law in Germany is regulated in the Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (Civil Code) and ensures that both spouses are treated as legal equals.
messages.glossary.Schengener Abkommen.context: The Schengen Agreement is an international treaty that most European states have joined and that creates a common travel area without passport controls at internal borders. Citizens can thereby travel between Schengen states without having to show their passport or identity document. The agreement is a central element of European integration and facilitates freedom of movement for people within Europe.
messages.glossary.Schöffe.context: A Schöffe is a volunteer lay judge who, without legal training, participates equally alongside professional judges in criminal proceedings and decides jointly with them on guilt and sentencing. Schöffen are elected for a fixed term of office and represent the democratic principle of citizen participation in the administration of justice.
messages.glossary.Schuld.context: In criminal law, guilt designates the legally established responsibility of a person for a committed offense. A court first examines whether the accused person is guilty before determining the sentence. This separation between establishing guilt and determining the sentence is a fundamental principle of criminal procedure under the rule of law.
messages.glossary.Schule.context: The school system in Germany is under state supervision in accordance with Article 7 of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). At public schools, religious instruction is required as a regular subject, unless the school is secular. In this way, the Grundgesetz links state responsibility for education with the constitutionally recognized role of religious communities.
messages.glossary.Schulpflicht.context: School attendance is compulsory and requires all children in Germany to attend a state-recognized school regularly. It is governed in the school laws of the individual Länder and typically begins at age six. As a state-established civic duty, it connects the right to education with the responsibility to ensure all children have equal access to it.
messages.glossary.Schulpolitik.context: School policy covers state decisions about school structures, curricula, types of schools, and teacher training. In Germany, this responsibility does not rest with the federal government but with the individual Länder – reflecting the federalism enshrined in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law). For this reason, the school system, including school certificates and curriculum content, can differ from one Land to another.
messages.glossary.Schulwesen.context: The school system comprises all public and private schools in a country, as well as the legal foundations for instruction, curricula, and school attendance. In Germany, the school system is, according to the Grundgesetz (Basic Law), a matter for the Länder; that is, each of the 16 Bundesländer regulates school structure and educational content independently. This division reflects the federal structure of the Federal Republic.
messages.glossary.Sektor.context: After German defeat in 1945, the four Allied powers – the United States, the Soviet Union, Great Britain, and France – divided Germany and Berlin each into occupation zones, or sectors. Each power administered its sector independently according to its own vision. Berlin thereby became a central flashpoint of the Cold War, as the different political systems of the Allied powers came into direct contact in the divided city.
messages.glossary.Senator.context: A senator in Berlin heads a particular policy area, such as finance, education, or interior affairs. The role is equivalent to that of a minister in other German Länder. Together with the governing mayor, the senators form the Berliner Senat (Berlin Senate), which constitutes the state government.
messages.glossary.Sitz.context: A seat in parliament represents the mandate of an elected representative and thus their right to participate in the parliament's votes and debates. In the Bundestag, the number of seats a party holds is determined by its share of the second votes – provided it has exceeded the five-percent threshold. This proportional representation system is intended to ensure that parliament's composition reflects the political will of the voters as accurately as possible.
messages.glossary.sowjetische Besatzungszone.cont: After the end of World War II in 1945, Germany was divided into four occupation zones; the eastern part was under Soviet control. From this Soviet occupation zone emerged the DDR (German Democratic Republic) in 1949. The DDR was organized as a socialist state modeled on the Soviet system and existed until German reunification in 1990.
messages.glossary.Sowjetunion.context: The Soviet Union was one of four Allied victor powers that divided and administered Germany into occupation zones after 1945. From the Soviet-controlled eastern zone emerged the DDR (German Democratic Republic) in 1949, where a socialist system modeled on the Soviet system was introduced, which sharply restricted fundamental rights and democratic participation. With the blockade of the land routes to West Berlin in 1948, the Soviet Union attempted to exert political pressure on the Western Allies.
messages.glossary.Sozialabgabe.context: Social contributions are legally mandated payments that employees and their employers make together to the providers of social insurance. They are deducted directly from wages and finance benefits such as health, pension, unemployment, and long-term care insurance. This contribution principle links individual payment with collective protection for all insured persons.
messages.glossary.Sozialdemokratische Partei Deut: The Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD) is one of the oldest political parties in Germany, founded in 1863. It occupies the left-center of the political spectrum and traditionally advocates values such as social justice, worker rights, and state-provided essential services. As one of the major parties, it is a central actor in political will-formation in the German party system.
messages.glossary.soziale Marktwirtschaft.context: Social market economy is the economic system of the Federal Republic of Germany: it combines free competition and private enterprise with state mechanisms of protection and adjustment. The state intervenes in regulation to cushion social inequality and secure essential provision – for example through social insurance, minimum wages, and transfer payments – without fundamentally eliminating market freedom. This economic model is closely linked to the social state principle enshrined in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law).
messages.glossary.soziale Sicherheit.context: Social security designates state-organized protection of the population from economic risks such as illness, unemployment, need for care, and old-age poverty. In Germany, it is realized through the social insurance system, which is based on the solidarity principle: employees and employers jointly pay contributions, from which benefits are financed in case of need. Social security is an essential feature of the welfare state principle, which is anchored in the Basic Law (Grundgesetz).
messages.glossary.sozialer Ausgleich.context: Social compensation refers to the principle of mitigating unacceptable economic inequalities through state intervention that arise from free market processes. The welfare state implements this principle through the social insurance system, taxes, and transfer payments such as unemployment benefits or housing allowance. In the Basic Law (Grundgesetz), the welfare state obligation is anchored as a state objective and thus forms one of the constitutional foundations for solidarity and social cohesion.
messages.glossary.Sozialstaat.context: The welfare state principle, anchored in Article 20 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz), obligates the state to ensure social justice and security for all citizens. It is based on the principle of solidarity: everyone contributes together to systems such as pension, health, unemployment, and long-term care insurance, so that each person receives support in case of need. Thus, the welfare state, alongside the rule of law principle and the democracy principle, forms a fundamental constitutional principle of the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.Sozialstaatsprinzip.context: The welfare state principle is anchored in Article 20 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) and obligates the state to ensure social justice and security for all people. It establishes a comprehensive system of social protection – such as through pension, health, and unemployment insurance – as well as the mandate for equal opportunity. This is not about an even distribution of income or assets, but rather about protection from existential risks and the preservation of human dignity.
messages.glossary.SPD.context: The Social Democratic Party of Germany (SPD) is one of the oldest German parties and is among the major people's parties with social democratic orientation. It stands for social justice, workers' rights, and state provision of essential services. Its party color is red.
messages.glossary.Staat.context: The state is the politically and legally organized community that regulates coexistence through its own institutions and laws and exercises sovereign authority. In Germany, the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) obligates the state to fulfill basic tasks: ensure security, provide infrastructure, protect fundamental rights, and secure welfare state services. These tasks are financed through taxes and levies of citizens, while the state remains ideologically neutral and favors no religion.
messages.glossary.Staat Israel.context: The State of Israel was founded in 1948 and is the only Jewish democracy in the world. Due to the Holocaust and German responsibility for the destruction of European Jewry, the Federal Republic of Germany considers the security of Israel as part of its reason of state – that is, as a fundamental principle of its foreign policy. This special responsibility is permanently anchored in Germany's historical and political self-understanding.
messages.glossary.Staatsangehöriger.context: A citizen is a person who, through birth, descent, or naturalization, legally belongs to a specific state. This belonging creates mutual rights and duties: the state grants protection and social security, while citizens fulfill certain civic duties. Personal life decisions such as leisure activities lie in the private sphere of the individual and are not a state responsibility.
messages.glossary.Staatsbürgerschaft.context: Citizenship designates the legal belonging of a person to the Federal Republic of Germany and is the basis for certain political rights. For example, active and passive voting rights in Bundestag (parliament) elections are tied to German nationality – foreign nationals are excluded from this, even if they reside permanently in Germany. Nationality is acquired through birth, descent, or naturalization.
messages.glossary.Staatsform.context: The form of government describes how a state is fundamentally organized and who holds supreme state authority. In a republic, this authority emanates from the people and is exercised through elected bodies – in contrast, for example, to a monarchy, in which a ruler holds state authority through hereditary succession. Germany has been anchored as a democratic republic in the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) since 1949, and this form of government is among the unchangeable fundamental principles of the Constitution.
messages.glossary.Staatsgewalt.context: State authority designates the sovereign power that a state exercises over all people in its territory. The Basic Law (Grundgesetz) divides it into three independent areas: legislative authority (Legislature), executive authority (Executive), and judicial authority (Judiciary). This separation of powers is intended to prevent abuse of power and ensure that state authority emanates solely from the people.
messages.glossary.Staatsoberhaupt.context: The head of state represents the state internally and externally and takes on primarily representative and integrative tasks. In Germany, this is the Bundespräsident (Federal President), who is elected by the Bundesversammlung (Federal Assembly). This role clearly differs from that of the Bundeskanzler (Federal Chancellor), who leads the government and determines the state's policy.
messages.glossary.Staatswappen der DDR.context: The state emblem of the DDR (German Democratic Republic) displayed a hammer, compass, and wreath of grain as symbols for the working class, intelligentsia, and peasantry. These three elements were meant to illustrate the alliance of social classes according to Marxist-Leninist ideology. The emblem was a central symbol of the DDR's socialist state order and deliberately distinguished it from the Federal Republic of Germany.
messages.glossary.Stadtstaat.context: A Stadtstaat (city-state) is a Bundesland (federal state) that is at the same time a single city and thus comprises no further municipalities or districts. Germany has three city-states: Berlin, Hamburg, and Bremen. Each city-state combines the functions of a state government and a city government in a single territorial authority and has its own Landesparlament (state parliament) and Landesregierung (state government).
messages.glossary.Standesamt.context: The Standesamt (registry office) is a municipal authority that legally documents life events such as birth, marriage, and death. In Germany, a marriage is legally valid only when conducted before the Standesamt—a purely religious or church ceremony has no legal effect. For marriages, birth certificates, and death certificates, the Standesamt is the responsible state authority.
messages.glossary.Stasi.context: The Stasi (Ministry for State Security) was the secret police of the DDR and monitored the population through a dense network of full-time officers and unofficial informants to persecute and suppress political opponents of the regime. As an instrument of SED (Socialist Unity Party) dictatorship, it embodied the systematic intrusion of the state into the private lives of citizens. After reunification, its files were made accessible and serve as significant historical testimony to state repression and violations of fundamental rights.
messages.glossary.Steuerbescheid.context: A tax assessment is a written administrative act by the tax office that bindingly establishes the tax liability of a person or business. It has legal effect but can be challenged through an objection to the tax office within a statutory period. Only after an unsuccessful objection is a lawsuit before a tax court possible.
messages.glossary.Stimmabgabe.context: Casting a vote is the formal act through which those entitled to vote exercise their vote in an election and thereby participate in democratic decision-making. In Germany, it is protected by the principle of the secret ballot: no one may know for whom a person has voted. This ballot secret ensures freedom of decision and protects voters from pressure or undue influence.
messages.glossary.Stimme.context: A vote is the personal ballot cast by an eligible voter in an election. In Bundestag (Federal Parliament) elections, each voter has two votes: the first vote (Erststimme) goes to a direct candidate in their district, the second vote (Zweitstimme) to a party list. The two-vote system combines the personal choice of individual candidates with proportional representation of parties in parliament.
messages.glossary.Stimmzettel.context: The ballot paper is the official document by which those entitled to vote cast their votes in elections. Its form and completion are regulated by law; ballot papers that deviate from these requirements can be declared invalid. It is thus a central instrument of secret and free voting in a democracy.
messages.glossary.Stolperstein.context: Stolpersteine (stumbling stones) are small brass plaques set into sidewalks in front of the last freely chosen residences of victims of National Socialism. Each stone bears the name, birth year, and fate of an individual person, thus individually commemorating the deported, murdered, and persecuted. The project, initiated by artist Gunter Demnig, is today the world's largest decentralized memorial.
messages.glossary.Strafe.context: A penalty is a legal consequence imposed by the state against persons who have violated applicable laws. In the German rule of law, only an independent court may impose a penalty; the type and degree of the penalty are determined by the criminal code. Penalties serve both the protection of society and the responsibility of individuals for their actions.
messages.glossary.Strafgesetz.context: A criminal law is a legal provision that classifies certain actions as criminal offenses and sets out the penalties or measures provided for them. In Germany, the fundamental criminal provisions are consolidated in the Strafgesetzbuch (criminal code), but criminal norms also exist in numerous other laws. Criminal laws apply to all criminally responsible persons and thus form a central component of the rule-of-law principle of binding to law.
messages.glossary.Strafmündigkeit.context: Criminal responsibility (Strafmündigkeit) is the legal capacity of a person to be held accountable for a criminal offense and prosecuted under criminal law. In Germany, it begins upon completion of the 14th year of life; younger children are considered incapable of criminal responsibility. For juveniles between 14 and 17 years of age, juvenile criminal law (Jugendstrafrecht) applies, which favors educational measures over punishment.
messages.glossary.Strafprozess.context: A criminal trial (Strafprozess) is a court procedure in which an independent court examines whether an accused person has committed a criminal offense. The procedure is regulated by law and protects fundamental rights: the accused has the right to defense, and the hearing is generally public. These principles ensure the rule of law's control over state action and protect against arbitrary punishment.
messages.glossary.Straftat.context: A criminal offense (Straftat) is an act that violates criminal law and therefore can be prosecuted and punished by the state. In a constitutional state (Rechtsstaat), the presumption of innocence applies: no one may be convicted solely on the basis of suspicion, but only after a lawful procedure before an independent court. Criminal law thus protects both society from harmful acts and individuals from arbitrary state power.
messages.glossary.Straße.context: Roads are part of public infrastructure and part of the state's responsibility for essential services. Their construction and maintenance are the responsibility of the federal government (Bund), the states (Länder), or local authorities (Kommunen), depending on their importance and location.
messages.glossary.Synagoge.context: A synagogue is a house of worship and religious community center of the Jewish population; it serves prayer, study of the Torah, and communal life. During the night of November 9–10, 1938, hundreds of synagogues throughout the German Reich were destroyed and set on fire by Nazis—an expression of state-organized persecution of the Jewish population. After 1945, many synagogues were rebuilt and stand today as places of Jewish life under special state protection.
messages.glossary.Tag der Deutschen Einheit.conte: German Unity Day (Tag der Deutschen Einheit) on October 3 is the national holiday of the Federal Republic of Germany. It commemorates October 3, 1990, when the German Democratic Republic (DDR) joined the Federal Republic of Germany and ended the country's more than 40-year division. As a state symbol of reunification, this date is established by law.
messages.glossary.Thüringen.context: Thuringia (Thüringen) is a state in the center of Germany. It is one of five states that were re-established in 1990 during reunification on the territory of the former German Democratic Republic (DDR). Before German division, Thuringia already existed as an independent state.
messages.glossary.Trennungsjahr.context: The separation year (Trennungsjahr) is a legal requirement for divorce in Germany, established in Section 1565 of the Civil Code (BGB). Spouses must live separately for at least one year before a family court may dissolve the marriage. This waiting period is intended to ensure that the marriage has genuinely failed permanently and there is no prospect of reconciliation.
messages.glossary.Umsatzsteuer.context: Sales tax (Umsatzsteuer) is a state levy on the purchase of goods and services that businesses collect at the point of sale and remit to the state. Economically, it is borne by the end consumer, as it is included in the price. It is an indirect tax and is not part of deductions from wages, which include income tax (Lohnsteuer) and social security contributions.
messages.glossary.Ungleichbehandlung.context: Unequal treatment (Ungleichbehandlung) refers to unjustified differential treatment of people by the state or its institutions. Article 3 of the Basic Law (Grundgesetz) requires all state bodies to treat every person equally before the law and to refrain from discrimination. This principle of equality protects against arbitrary state action and is one of the fundamental pillars of the rule of law principle.
messages.glossary.Urteil.context: A judgment (Urteil) is a binding court decision at the end of a proceeding. In Germany, it is rendered in the name of the people, expressing that the judiciary derives its legitimacy from the democratic community. Judgments may determine criminal guilt, penalties, or civil claims and are an expression of the rule of law principle, under which conflicts are resolved by independent courts according to established rules.
messages.glossary.Verband.context: An association (Verband) is an organized union of individuals, businesses, or organizations with common interests or goals. Associations represent the interests of their members to the state, economy, and society and are an important part of civil society. Typical examples are professional associations, welfare associations, and sports associations, which are often built on voluntary work.
messages.glossary.Verein.context: An association is a voluntary group of people who pursue a common goal over time—for example in sport, culture, social services, or politics. In Germany, associations are one of the most important forms of civic engagement and form the backbone of civil society. The right to found associations is protected in the Grundgesetz (Basic Law) as freedom of association.
messages.glossary.Vereinte Nationen.context: The United Nations (UN) is an international organization founded in 1945 and includes nearly all states of the world. It promotes peace, international cooperation, and respect for international law. Its principal bodies—the General Assembly and the Security Council—can pass resolutions that have legal force under international law and can regulate state and territorial questions at the international level.
messages.glossary.Verfassungsorgan.context: Constitutional organs are the highest state organs of the Bundesrepublik Deutschland (Federal Republic of Germany) explicitly established in the Grundgesetz, each with its own powers. They include the Bundestag, Bundesrat, Bundesregierung (Federal Government), Bundespräsident (Federal President), and Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court). They work together in the formation of the state's political will, in legislation, and in mutual oversight.
messages.glossary.verfassungswidrig.context: Unconstitutional refers to anything that violates the fundamental principles of the Grundgesetz. The Grundgesetz establishes the free democratic basic order, which must be respected by both state organs and citizens alike. Laws, actions, or organizations that endanger this basic order or seek to abolish it can be declared unconstitutional and banned by the Bundesverfassungsgericht (Federal Constitutional Court).
messages.glossary.Vergebung.context: In a political and historical context, forgiveness refers to a public acknowledgment of wrongs committed and a willingness to reconcile between perpetrator and victim societies. It is a central element of memorial culture and serves to address historical guilt, especially in relations with peoples who suffered under Nazi rule. Such gestures create a moral foundation for normalizing relations between nations and promote lasting peace.
messages.glossary.Verhaftung.context: An arrest is the temporary deprivation of personal freedom by state authorities. The Grundgesetz protects freedom of the person and establishes that deprivation of freedom is only permitted on a legal basis and, as a rule, with a court order. This is intended to prevent arbitrary detention by the state.
messages.glossary.Verhältniswahlrecht.context: In proportional representation, the number of parliamentary seats a party receives is determined by its percentage share of the votes cast. This ensures that the composition of parliament reflects the voters' will as accurately as possible. In Germany, this principle applies to the second vote (Zweitstimme), which determines the distribution of seats in the Bundestag.
messages.glossary.Vernichtung.context: In the German historical context, annihilation refers to the systematic extermination of people, particularly in connection with the Nazi Holocaust, in which six million Jews were murdered. Calls for the annihilation of specific groups or states draw on this ideology of annihilation and are punishable under German law. The Grundgesetz and German legal order explicitly protect the dignity of all people and prohibit incitement aimed at the extermination of groups or peoples.
messages.glossary.Vernichtungslager.context: Extermination camps were facilities of the Nazi regime erected specifically for the systematic mass murder of people—primarily Jews as part of the Holocaust. Auschwitz-Birkenau was the largest of these camps and stands as a central symbol of the industrially organized genocide of European Jews. The liberation of the camps by the Allies at the end of World War II revealed the scale of Nazi crimes and fundamentally shaped the international human rights order of the postwar period.
messages.glossary.Verwaltungsaufbau.context: The administrative structure in Germany describes the division of state functions across three levels: Bund (federal level), Länder (states), and Gemeinden (municipalities). Each level is responsible for specific areas of public administration and acts within the powers assigned to it by the Grundgesetz. This federal principle distributes responsibility and prevents the concentration of power in a single authority.
messages.glossary.Volk.context: In German constitutional law, 'the people' (Volk) refers to all citizens collectively as the bearers of political sovereignty. According to the Grundgesetz (Basic Law), all state power emanates from the people—a principle known as popular sovereignty. The people exercise this power primarily through free elections and referendums.
messages.glossary.Volksaufstand.context: A popular uprising is a spontaneous or organized revolt by large sections of the population against a state authority or political wrongs. The popular uprising of 17 June 1953 in the DDR (German Democratic Republic) emerged from workers' resistance to state-mandated production quotas and quickly expanded into broad social protest against the SED regime. Soviet troops violently suppressed the uprising, which made clear the DDR's dependence on the Soviet Union and the regime's lack of democratic legitimacy.
messages.glossary.Volkshochschule.context: The Volkshochschule (VHS, public adult education center) is a publicly funded institution of adult education present in nearly all German cities and municipalities. It offers courses in areas such as languages, health, profession, and social participation — open to all adults regardless of educational background or origin. As part of the state-supported education system, it embodies the principle of lifelong learning.
messages.glossary.Volkssouveränität.context: Volkssouveränität (popular sovereignty) designates the constitutional principle that all state power emanates from the people — anchored in Article 20 of the Grundgesetz (German constitution). The people exercise this power through elections and referendums as well as through the organs of legislation, executive power, and judiciary. Thus, the people are the sole source of political legitimation in the democratic state.
messages.glossary.Volljährigkeit.context: On reaching the age of 18 in Germany, a person attains Volljährigkeit (legal adulthood), regulated in the Bürgerliches Gesetzbuch (BGB, German Civil Code). From that point on, individuals are fully capable of legal action, parental custody ends, and they bear full legal responsibility for their decisions. Legal adulthood is also a prerequisite for the right to vote.
messages.glossary.Wahlalter.context: Voting age specifies at what age citizens may participate in political elections. In Germany, the right to vote for the Bundestag is linked to reaching the age of 18, which coincides with the attainment of legal adulthood. With legal adulthood, people assume legal responsibility for themselves, which is why this age is considered a requirement for political participation.
messages.glossary.Wahlbenachrichtigung.context: A voting notification is an official letter that eligible voters receive from their municipality before an election. It contains information about the responsible polling place, voting time, and the option to apply for postal voting. The document serves as proof of voting eligibility and facilitates practical participation in elections.
messages.glossary.Wahlberechtigung.context: Voting eligibility designates the right to vote, that is, the right of a person to participate in an election and cast a ballot. For Bundestag elections, it requires German citizenship and reaching the age of 18. The principle that the enfranchised people elect their representatives is an expression of Volkssouveränität (popular sovereignty) anchored in the Grundgesetz (German constitution).
messages.glossary.Wählermehrheit.context: In a democracy, the electoral majority determines which party or coalition forms the government. This majority principle ensures that political power emanates from the people and is regularly redistributed. With each election, the majority can confirm an existing government or replace it with a new one.
messages.glossary.Wählerstimme.context: In a Bundestag election, each eligible voter has two votes: the first vote for a direct candidate in the constituency and the second vote for a party. The second votes determine the distribution of seats in the Bundestag and form the basis for calculating the five-percent threshold. Parties that do not exceed this threshold generally receive no seats in Parliament.
messages.glossary.Wahlgeheimnis.context: Ballot secrecy guarantees that the voting of every eligible voter remains confidential and cannot be viewed by other citizens or state authorities. It protects voters from pressure, intimidation, or disadvantage based on their political decision. As a constitutional principle, it is an essential condition for elections to take place freely and without distortion.
messages.glossary.Wahlgesetz.context: Electoral law regulates the conduct of elections and specifies under what conditions parties may enter a parliament. For the German Bundestag, the Bundeswahlgesetz (Federal Electoral Code) applies, which concretizes the electoral principles anchored in the Grundgesetz (German constitution). It includes, among other things, the five-percent threshold, which is intended to ensure that the parliament remains functional.
messages.glossary.Wahlhelfer.context: Poll workers are citizens who voluntarily support the conduct of elections in polling places — from supervising voting operations to counting ballots. They are members of a polling board that is responsible for proper conduct of the election locally. Because free and fair elections require adequate staff, German electoral law provides for a duty of citizens to assume this office.
messages.glossary.Wahlrecht.context: Voting rights regulate who may participate in elections and under what conditions. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) specifies in Article 38 that elections must be universal, direct, free, equal, and secret. Voting in Germany is voluntary — there is no legal voting obligation.
messages.glossary.Wahlsystem.context: The electoral system determines how votes are converted into political mandates according to which rules. Germany uses personalized proportional representation: voters cast two votes — one for a person in their constituency and one for a party — and the distribution of seats in parliament overall reflects party strengths. This system combines the direct citizen connection of constituency mandates with proportional representation of social forces.
messages.glossary.Wahlzwang.context: In Germany there is no voting obligation — voting is voluntary. This principle follows from the freedom principle of the Grundgesetz (Basic Law): voting is a right, not a compulsory duty. Those who do not vote act without civic engagement, but face no legal consequences.
messages.glossary.Wappentier.context: A heraldic animal is the animal depicted in a state coat of arms that symbolically represents a country or institution. The coat of arms of the Federal Republic of Germany shows a black eagle on a gold field — the Bundesadler (Federal Eagle). It originates from centuries-old heraldic tradition and is considered one of Germany's official state symbols.
messages.glossary.Warschauer Pakt.context: The Warsaw Pact was a military alliance of socialist states founded in 1955 under Soviet leadership, to which the DDR (East Germany), Poland, and Czechoslovakia belonged, among others. It was created as the eastern counterpart to NATO and placed the armed forces of member states under Soviet command. With the end of the Cold War and the collapse of socialist governments in Eastern Europe, the alliance was dissolved in 1991.
messages.glossary.Weihnachten.context: Christmas is the Christian holiday commemorating the birth of Jesus Christ and is celebrated in Germany on 24–26 December. It is one of the most important cultural holidays in the country, whose customs — such as decorating a Christmas tree or exchanging gifts — are widespread far beyond religious circles. As statutory public holidays, 25 and 26 December are firmly established in public life.
messages.glossary.Weiterbildung.context: Further education refers to the continuation or resumption of organized learning after an initial educational phase in adulthood. It encompasses vocational training, general education, and civic education equally. In Germany, further education is regarded as an important part of the education system and is promoted through state and municipal programs.
messages.glossary.Widerspruch.context: An objection is a formal legal remedy with which citizens can challenge an administrative decision for reconsideration by the administration. It must be filed in writing and generally has suspensory effect — that is, the decision is initially not implemented. The objection procedure is regulated by law and in many cases forms the necessary preliminary stage before a lawsuit at an administrative court.
messages.glossary.Wiedervereinigung.context: Reunification refers to the process by which the DDR (East Germany) joined the Federal Republic of Germany on 3 October 1990, overcoming the division of Germany that had existed since 1949. Five new Bundesländer (federal states) were created on the territory of the former DDR. The Two-plus-Four Treaty secured the full sovereignty of reunified Germany and its state borders under international law.
messages.glossary.Willy Brandt.context: Willy Brandt (1913–1992) was Chancellor of the Federal Republic of Germany from 1969 to 1974. He shaped the so-called Neue Ostpolitik (New Eastern Policy), a foreign policy strategy of rapprochement with the states of Eastern Europe and recognition of postwar borders. For his services to détente in Europe, he received the Nobel Peace Prize in 1971.
messages.glossary.Wirtschaftsform.context: An economic system describes the basic organizational principle according to which production, distribution, and consumption are regulated in a state. Germany follows the model of the social market economy, which combines free competition with state social responsibility. This principle ensures economic freedom and simultaneously protects the population from social risks.
messages.glossary.Wirtschaftswunder.context: The term Wirtschaftswunder (economic miracle) refers to the rapid economic reconstruction and strong growth of the Federal Republic of Germany in the 1950s following the destruction of World War II. The currency reform of 1948, the US Marshall Plan, and economic policy based on free market principles created the conditions for a substantial increase in production, employment, and standard of living. This reconstruction phase laid the material foundation for a stable democratic society in the early Federal Republic.
messages.glossary.Wohnort.context: A place of residence is the location where a person has their permanent center of life. In Germany, the fundamental right of Freizügigkeit (freedom of movement) protects the free choice of residence—no one needs administrative permission for it. This right applies to all people with legal residence in the federal territory.
messages.glossary.Zwangsarbeit.context: Forced labor refers to work that is imposed on a person under threat of punishment or violence and in which no free choice is possible. Under Nazism, forced labor was systematically and extensively used, especially against persecuted population groups and prisoners of war. The Grundgesetz (Basic Law) explicitly prohibits forced labor in Article 12 and guarantees freedom of occupation as a response to these historical experiences.
messages.glossary.Zweiter Weltkrieg.context: World War II was a global war triggered by Nazi Germany, which began on September 1, 1939 with the German invasion of Poland and ended in Europe on May 8, 1945 with the unconditional surrender of the German Wehrmacht (armed forces). It claimed over 60 million deaths worldwide, including six million Jews murdered in the Holocaust. Germany's complete defeat led to the division of the country into occupation zones and formed the foundation for the democratic fresh start of the Federal Republic and the commitment to respect fundamental rights.
messages.glossary.Zweitstimme.context: In elections to the Bundestag, each person eligible to vote has two votes. The Zweitstimme (second vote) goes to a party and determines, according to proportional representation, how many seats that party receives in the Bundestag. It is thus the decisive vote for the composition of parliament.
